nephesh (soul/life/self -- 'the soul of Jonathan was knit to the soul of David' (18:1). Nephesh is the total person, not a disembodied 'spirit.' The bonding of two nephesh-ot is the deepest connection between persons -- a merger of life-commitments, not merely emotional affection)

hesed (covenant loyalty/lovingkindness -- Jonathan asks David to show 'the hesed of YHWH' to his house forever (20:14-15). Hesed is not mere kindness but the covenant obligation to act faithfully toward those bound to you by oath. David will fulfill this toward Mephibosheth in 2 Samuel 9)

chapters.title: Nob, the Cave of Adullam, and Saul Spared at En-gedi
chapters.synopsis: David flees to Nob, where the priest Ahimelech gives him the bread of the Presence (lechem hapanim) and Goliath's sword (21:1-9). This episode becomes a key text when Jesus cites it to justify his dis
hebrew_terms: lechem hapanim (bread of the Presence / showbread -- literally 'bread of the face,' referring to the twelve loaves placed on the golden table in the tabernacle each Sabbath (Lev 24:5-9). The twelve loaves represented the twelve tribes perpetually before YHWH's 'face' (panim). Normally only priests could eat the old bread when it was replaced. Ahimelech's giving it to David under emergency conditions established a precedent that Jesus later cites (Mark 2:25-26) to teach that human need can override ceremonial regulation: 'The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath')

mashiach YHWH (YHWH's anointed -- the sacrosanct title David refuses to violate, even for self-preservation. The mashiach is one upon whom YHWH has poured anointing oil, marking him as divinely commissioned. David uses this title for Saul repeatedly (24:6, 10; 26:9, 11, 16, 23) even when Saul is trying to kill him. The principle: YHWH installs and YHWH removes. No human may preempt YHWH's sovereign decisions about his anointed agents. This theology governs the entire Davidic narrative and ultimately applies to Christ, the final Mashiach)

kanaf (corner/wing/edge -- David cuts the kanaf of Saul's robe at En-gedi (24:4). The word means both 'corner' of a garment and 'wing.' Ruth asks Boaz to spread his kanaf over her (Ruth 3:9) as a symbol of marriage and protection; the Psalms celebrate refuge 'under YHWH's wings (kanaf)' (Ps 17:8; 36:7; 91:4). David cuts Saul's royal kanaf -- symbolically trimming the king's authority -- and immediately his 'heart struck him' (24:5). Even this symbolic act against YHWH's anointed troubles David's conscience)

me'arah (cave -- the cave of Adullam becomes David's headquarters and refuge. The Judean wilderness contains hundreds of caves used as shelters from antiquity. Several psalms are attributed to David's cave experiences: Psalm 57 ('Be merciful to me, O God... in the shadow of your wings I take refuge') and Psalm 142 ('When my spirit faints within me, you know my way'). The caves become the birthplace of Israel's worship poetry)

ephod (priestly oracle garment -- when Abiathar flees to David with the ephod (23:6), David gains access to YHWH's direct guidance. The ephod was a priestly vest containing the Urim and Thummim -- oracle stones or devices used to inquire of YHWH with yes/no questions (cf. Exod 28:30; Num 27:21). David 'inquires of YHWH' through the ephod at critical moments (23:9-12; 30:7-8), receiving specific tactical guidance. This access to YHWH's direction distinguishes David from Saul, who eventually cannot get YHWH to answer him at all (28:6))

habiru / na'ar (outlaws-refugees / young men -- David's 400 men (later 600) are described as 'everyone who was in distress, and everyone who was in debt, and everyone who was bitter in soul' (22:2). They parallel the habiru described in the Amarna Letters: displaced persons, social outcasts, and fugitives who formed armed bands in the margins of society. David transforms these marginalized men into the nucleus of his royal guard and Israel's greatest warriors (the 'Mighty Men' of 2 Sam 23))

chapters.title: Abigail, Saul Spared Again, and David Among the Philistines
chapters.synopsis: Samuel dies and all Israel mourns (25:1). David encounters the wealthy Nabal ('Fool') and his wise wife Abigail. When Nabal refuses to provision David's men despite their protection of his flocks, Dav
hebrew_terms: naval (fool/senseless -- Nabal's name and character. His wife Abigail explains: 'As his name is, so is he. Naval is his name, and folly (nevalah) is with him' (25:25). In Hebrew, naval is not merely unintelligent but morally deficient: the 'fool' who says 'there is no God' in Psalm 14:1 is the naval. Nabal's refusal to provision David is not just rudeness but a denial of YHWH's providence working through David)

bayit ne'eman (sure/faithful house -- Abigail's prophecy that YHWH will establish David's dynasty: 'YHWH will certainly make my lord a sure house (bayit ne'eman)' (25:28). This anticipates the Davidic Covenant of 2 Samuel 7 by several chapters. Abigail speaks prophetically -- she announces the dynasty before Nathan delivers the formal covenant oracle. The adjective ne'eman means 'established, reliable, trustworthy' -- what YHWH builds endures)

tsror hachayyim (bundle of the living -- Abigail's remarkable metaphor: 'the life (nephesh) of my lord shall be bound in the bundle of the living (tsror hachayyim) in the care of YHWH your God' (25:29). The image is of a precious object tied securely in a bundle for safekeeping. This phrase became the standard Jewish memorial inscription on tombstones to this day. It may reference a heavenly registry where the righteous are preserved (cf. Dan 12:1, 'everyone whose name is written in the book'; Phil 4:3, 'the book of life'))

chanit (spear -- Saul's signature weapon and the symbol of his kingship (cf. 1 Sam 18:10-11; 19:9-10; 22:6 where Saul sits 'with his spear in his hand'). When David takes Saul's spear at Hachilah (26:12) and displays it as proof of mercy, he is holding the very weapon Saul has hurled at him repeatedly. The spear embodies Saul's violent, self-protective kingship -- and David returns it rather than use it)

tardemah (deep sleep -- YHWH casts a tardemah on Saul's camp so David can enter (26:12). This is the same word used for Adam's sleep when YHWH created Eve (Gen 2:21) and Abraham's sleep during the covenant ceremony (Gen 15:12). The tardemah is divine intervention -- YHWH creating conditions for his purposes while the human participants are unconscious. It is not natural sleep but supernatural incapacitation)

Ziklag (the Philistine town given to David by Achish of Gath as his vassal base for 16 months (27:5-7). Archaeological identification is debated: Tell esh-Shari'a or Tell es-Seba in the northern Negev. David's period at Ziklag is morally complex: he lives as a Philistine vassal while secretly raiding Israel's enemies and deceiving his overlord. The narrator reports this without moral commentary, letting the reader assess David's compromises during the darkest phase of his fugitive years)

chapters.title: Endor, Ziklag, and the Death of Saul
chapters.synopsis: The Philistines gather for a massive campaign against Israel. Achish enlists David, but the Philistine commanders refuse to trust him (29:3-4), sending him home to Ziklag -- which David finds burned b
hebrew_terms: 'ob (spirit of the dead / necromantic pit -- the technical term for the spirit-entity or the ritual mechanism by which the dead are contacted. The 'ob may refer to the spirit itself, the ritual pit through which spirits ascend, or the practitioner's familiar spirit. The word is cognate with Mesopotamian concepts of a spirit-hole connecting the world of the living to the underworld. Consulting an 'ob is strictly prohibited in the Torah (Lev 19:31; 20:6, 27; Deut 18:11) -- not because it does not work, but because it transgresses the boundary between the living and the dead that YHWH established)

ba'alat 'ob (mistress/possessor of an 'ob -- literally 'female master of a spirit-pit.' The woman at Endor is a professional necromancer (one who consults the dead). The term ba'al/ba'alat means 'owner/master' -- she controls or possesses the means of accessing the dead. Saul himself had banished all mediums and necromancers from Israel (28:3), making his resort to one the ultimate indicator of spiritual collapse: the king who enforced the law now violates it in desperation)

'elohim (divine being/spirit -- the woman sees 'an 'elohim ascending out of the earth' (28:13). Here 'elohim does not mean 'God' (YHWH) but 'a divine/spiritual being.' The word 'elohim in Hebrew is broader than English 'God': it can refer to YHWH, to angelic beings, to members of the divine council, and here to the departed dead who have entered the spiritual realm. The dead Samuel is an 'elohim because he now belongs to the spirit world. This usage is critical for understanding the biblical concept of the divine council: 'elohim is a category of being (spiritual/supernatural), not a unique title)

me'il (robe/mantle -- Samuel's prophetic garment, recognizable even in death: 'An old man is coming up, and he is wrapped in a me'il' (28:14). This is the same robe whose corner Saul tore in 15:27, prompting Samuel to declare: 'YHWH has torn the kingdom of Israel from you.' The me'il identifies Samuel across the boundary of death -- his prophetic identity persists beyond the grave)

Sheol (the realm of the dead -- the place where all the dead go in Old Testament theology. Sheol is not 'hell' in the later Christian sense but the underworld abode of the dead -- a shadowy, joyless place (Ps 88:3-6; Isa 14:9-11). Samuel 'comes up' from Sheol (28:15). His statement 'tomorrow you and your sons shall be with me' (28:19) means Saul will join the dead in Sheol. The Old Testament concept of the afterlife develops gradually; at this stage, Sheol is the common destination of all the dead, righteous and wicked alike)

vayitchazzeq (he strengthened himself -- 'David strengthened himself in YHWH his God' (30:6). When Ziklag is burned and his men talk of stoning him, David does not panic, blame others, or resort to illegitimate means. He strengthens himself in YHWH -- the spiritual core of the fugitive narrative. This is the opposite of Saul's response to crisis: where Saul consults a medium, David consults YHWH through the ephod. The contrast defines the difference between the two kings)

naphal (to fall -- Saul 'fell full length on the ground' in terror after Samuel's pronouncement (28:20), and then 'fell on his own sword' on Mount Gilboa (31:4). The verb naphal frames Saul's final hours: he falls before the dead prophet, and he falls on his own weapon. The man who stood 'head and shoulders above all the people' (9:2) ends face-down in the dirt)

chapters.title: Lament, Hebron, and the Civil War
chapters.synopsis: An Amalekite messenger brings news of Saul's death to David at Ziklag, claiming to have killed Saul at his request. David's response is not relief but grief and outrage. He orders the Amalekite execut
hebrew_terms: qinah (lament/dirge -- the formal funeral poem. The qinah has a distinctive Hebrew meter: a 3:2 beat pattern, where the second half of each line is shorter than the first. The effect is a rhythmic 'limping' -- the poem musically enacts the incompleteness of grief, each line trailing off before reaching resolution. David's lament for Saul and Jonathan (1:17-27) is the finest example of the genre in the Hebrew Bible)

qeshet (bow -- 'The Song of the Bow' (qeshet) is David's elegy title (1:18). Jonathan was an archer -- his weapon (the bow he gave David in 18:4) identifies him in this poem. The instruction 'to teach the people of Judah the Bow' (1:18) means the poem was to be memorized and sung publicly. It was recorded in the 'Book of Jashar' (sefer hayashar), a lost collection of Israel's heroic poetry also referenced in Joshua 10:13)

mashach (to anoint -- David is anointed king over Judah at Hebron (2:4), his second anointing. His first was by Samuel at Bethlehem (1 Sam 16:13); his third will be over all Israel (2 Sam 5:3). Three anointings for three stages of authority. Each anointing is by a different agent (prophet, tribe, nation) but the same oil symbolizes the same reality: YHWH's Spirit empowering for kingship)

Ish-bosheth (man of shame -- probably originally Ish-ba'al, 'man of Baal.' Later scribes replaced the 'Baal' element with bosheth ('shame') to avoid writing a pagan deity's name. The same scribal practice changed Mephi-ba'al to Mephi-bosheth and Jerub-ba'al to Jerubbesheth. The original name 'man of Baal' may have been innocuous -- ba'al simply means 'lord/master' -- but post-exile scribes were scrupulous about eliminating even ambiguous associations with Baal worship)

dam / damim (blood / bloodguilt -- David consistently refuses to benefit from bloodshed. He executes the Amalekite who claims to have killed Saul (1:14-16), publicly mourns Abner (3:31-39), and executes Ish-bosheth's assassins (4:9-12). The pattern establishes a theological principle: legitimate kingship does not advance through murder. YHWH's anointed waits for YHWH to remove obstacles -- he does not remove them himself. This ethic is unique in ANE political history)

Hebron (David's first capital -- located in the Judean hill country, about 19 miles south of Jerusalem. Hebron was one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the region (Num 13:22: 'built seven years before Zoan in Egypt'). Abraham settled at Hebron (Gen 13:18), purchased the cave of Machpelah there as a family tomb (Gen 23), and it became the patriarchal burial site. David's choice of Hebron as capital anchored his rule in patriarchal tradition and established continuity with the Abrahamic covenant)

chapters.title: Jerusalem Conquered, the Ark Comes Home
chapters.synopsis: All the tribes of Israel come to David at Hebron: 'Behold, we are your bone and flesh... YHWH said to you, You shall be shepherd of my people Israel, and you shall be prince (nagid) over Israel' (5:1-
hebrew_terms: ir David (City of David -- Jerusalem, David's newly conquered capital. The choice was politically brilliant: Jerusalem belonged to no Israelite tribe (it was a Jebusite city, Judg 1:21), making it neutral ground that would not provoke tribal jealousy. It sat on the border between Judah and Benjamin, bridging north and south. David renamed it 'the City of David,' making it his personal possession and the center of both political and spiritual authority when the Ark arrived)

tsinnor (water shaft/tunnel -- the means by which David's men captured the Jebusite fortress (5:8). The Jebusites boasted 'the blind and the lame will turn you back' -- their city was considered impregnable. Warren's Shaft, discovered in Jerusalem in 1867, was long thought to be the tsinnor David exploited, though recent archaeology complicates this identification. The point of the narrative: human fortifications cannot stand against YHWH's purpose)

emeq Rephaim (Valley of Rephaim/Giants -- the battle site southwest of Jerusalem where David twice defeated the Philistines (5:17-25). The valley's name connects to the Rephaim giant lineage (Gen 14:5; Deut 2:11, 20). David's victories here continue the pattern of YHWH's anointed defeating the seed of the rebellious bene ha'elohim. The Valley of Rephaim is the valley of the giants -- and YHWH's king conquers it)

perets (breach/breaking through -- David names the battle site Baal-perazim: 'YHWH has broken through (parats) my enemies before me, like a breaking forth of waters' (5:20). The image is of a dam bursting: YHWH's power smashes through opposition like floodwaters through a wall. David burns the Philistine idols left on the field -- the false 'elohim are destroyed alongside their armies)

tsa'adah (marching/stepping -- in the second Rephaim battle, YHWH tells David: 'When you hear the sound of marching (tsa'adah) in the tops of the balsam trees, then rouse yourself, for then YHWH has gone out before you to strike down the army of the Philistines' (5:24). The sound in the treetops is the heavenly army advancing. The divine host fights alongside the human army -- David's troops are the visible component of an invisible battle force. This is divine council warfare made audible)

ephod bad (linen ephod -- the priestly garment David wears while dancing before the Ark (6:14). The ephod bad was the standard priestly garment (1 Sam 2:18; 22:18). David, the king, wears the priest's garment while leading worship -- claiming a priestly function alongside his royal one. This foreshadows the Messiah who is both king and priest (Ps 110:4; Heb 7). Michal's contempt (6:16, 20) is not merely about David's undignified dancing but about her rejection of this priestly-royal worship)

chapters.title: The Davidic Covenant -- The Eternal Throne Promise
chapters.synopsis: This is the theological center of the entire Samuel-Kings narrative and arguably the most important messianic text in the Old Testament. David, now at rest from his enemies, notices a discrepancy: 'I
hebrew_terms: bayit (house -- the master word of the entire chapter, used in three senses: (1) bayit as temple -- David wants to build YHWH a 'house' to dwell in; (2) bayit as dynasty -- YHWH will build David a 'house' (royal line); (3) bayit as temple again -- David's seed will build YHWH a 'house' (Solomon's Temple). The triple wordplay is the literary and theological genius of the oracle: David cannot give YHWH a house; YHWH gives David a house that will then give YHWH a house. The initiative is always YHWH's)

zera (seed/offspring -- 'I will raise up your seed (zera) after you, who shall come from your body' (7:12). This word deliberately echoes the chain of seed promises running through the Bible: the seed of the woman who will crush the serpent (Gen 3:15), the seed of Abraham through whom all nations will be blessed (Gen 22:18), the seed of Judah who will hold the scepter (Gen 49:10). The Davidic 'seed' is the next link in this chain -- and the chain will not be complete until Christ, 'the Son of David' (Matt 1:1))

av / ben (father / son -- 'I will be to him a father (av), and he shall be to me a son (ben)' (7:14). This is divine adoption language: the Davidic king enters a unique filial relationship with YHWH. He is not divine by nature (as in Egyptian theology, where Pharaoh was literally 'son of Re') but adopted by decree. Psalm 2:7 makes this explicit: 'You are my Son; today I have begotten you' -- the coronation formula declaring adoption at enthronement. The New Testament applies this father-son language to Jesus as the ultimate 'Son of David' and 'Son of God' (Heb 1:5))

kisse (throne -- 'I will establish the throne (kisse) of his kingdom forever' (7:13). The kisse is the seat of royal authority, the physical symbol of sovereignty. YHWH promises to establish David's throne 'forever' (olam) -- a duration no mortal dynasty can fulfill. The promise inherently points beyond any single king to an eternal ruler. Jesus is proclaimed as the one who sits on 'the throne of his father David' (Luke 1:32))

olam (forever/eternity -- appears three times in the climactic verse: 'your house (bayit) forever... your kingdom (mamlakah) forever... your throne (kisse) forever' (7:16). Three olam-promises for three aspects of the Davidic inheritance. The word olam means 'to the vanishing point' -- a duration beyond what the eye can see. When applied to a covenant, it means 'without revocation.' The Davidic Covenant cannot expire)

hesed (covenant loyalty/lovingkindness -- 'my hesed I will not take from him, as I took it from Saul' (7:15). Hesed is not mere kindness but the obligation to act faithfully toward those bound to you by covenant. YHWH's hesed toward David's line is unconditional in duration (it will never be taken away) but conditional in experience (disobedient kings will be disciplined 'with the rod of men'). The covenant survives the man's failure because it rests on YHWH's faithfulness, not the king's perfection)

nagid (prince/ruler/designated leader -- 'I took you from the pasture... to be nagid over my people Israel' (7:8). The term nagid is deliberately used instead of melek (king): David is YHWH's appointed leader, but YHWH himself is the true melek (king) of Israel. The nagid governs on behalf of the cosmic sovereign. This preserves the theocratic principle: Israel's human king is always under YHWH's authority, never autonomous)

chapters.title: David's Empire and Covenant Faithfulness to Jonathan's House
chapters.synopsis: David's military campaigns establish Israel's greatest territorial extent: he defeats the Philistines, Moabites, Zobah, Arameans, Edomites, and Ammonites (ch. 8). He places garrisons in Edom and Aram
hebrew_terms: chesed Elohim (the kindness/hesed of God -- David's remarkable question: 'Is there still anyone left of the house of Saul, that I may show him the chesed of God (chesed Elohim)?' (9:3). David does not show merely human kindness but 'the hesed of God' -- divine covenant loyalty channeled through human action. In ANE politics, a new dynasty typically exterminated the previous royal house to prevent rival claims. David does the opposite: he seeks out the last heir of his rival's house to honor him. This is grace -- unmerited favor to one who might be considered an enemy)

shulchan hamelek (the king's table -- Mephibosheth is given a permanent place at the king's table: 'he ate at David's table, like one of the king's sons' (9:11). The king's table was the center of royal fellowship: to eat there meant inclusion in the royal family's daily life, provision, and protection. Mephibosheth, lame in both feet, coming from Lo-debar ('no pasture/no word' -- a place of desolation), is brought to the king's table as a son. From desolation to royal fellowship: this is the gospel in miniature)

mishpat utsedaqah (justice and righteousness -- 'David administered justice (mishpat) and righteousness (tsedaqah) to all his people' (8:15). These twin terms define ideal governance throughout the Old Testament. Mishpat is equitable legal judgment; tsedaqah is conformity to YHWH's standard of what is right. When both are present, the kingdom flourishes. When either is absent, the kingdom decays. The prophets will later judge every king by this standard: 'Let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream' (Amos 5:24))

netsivim (garrisons -- military outposts David places in conquered territories (Edom, Aram-Damascus). The garrisons ensure political control and tribute collection. David's empire at this point extends from the Euphrates approaches to the border of Egypt -- approximately the territory YHWH promised Abraham (Gen 15:18). This is the high-water mark of Israelite territorial control, never to be repeated after Solomon's reign)

nachalah (inheritance/allotted territory -- though not explicitly used in this passage, the concept underlies David's conquests. Each conquered territory (Philistia, Moab, Ammon, Edom, Aram) was the domain of a rival 'elohim under the Deuteronomy 32:8-9 allotment. David's expansion is YHWH reclaiming territory from the rebellious sons of God. The theological geography of the divine council explains why David's military conquests are simultaneously spiritual victories)

chapters.title: David and Bathsheba — Sin, Cover-Up, and Repentance
chapters.synopsis: The narrator’s opening is deliberately damning: ‘In the spring of the year, the time when kings go out to battle, David sent Joab... but David remained at Jerusalem’ (11:1). The king
hebrew_terms: chata (to sin — David’s confession: ‘I have sinned against YHWH’; the first step of repentance)
nasa (to put away/forgive — ‘YHWH has put away (he’evir) your sin’; the sin is transferred, not ignored)
kivsat harash (the poor man’s lamb — Nathan’s parable; Bathsheba as the ‘one little ewe lamb’)
cherev (sword — ‘the sword shall never depart from your house’; the consequence of bloodguilt)
Yedidyah (Beloved of YHWH — Nathan’s prophetic name for Solomon; YHWH’s grace in the aftermath of judgment)
ta’avah (desire/lust — the root of David’s sin; he took what was not his, violating the tenth commandment)

chapters.title: Amnon, Tamar, and Absalom’s Revenge
chapters.synopsis: The sword that Nathan promised begins to fall within David’s own household. Amnon, David’s firstborn son, becomes obsessed with his half-sister Tamar, Absalom’s full sister. His cous
hebrew_terms: nevalah (outrageous thing/folly — a technical term for a scandalous sexual violation that shocks the moral order; NOT the modern English word ‘folly’ (a silly mistake). In Hebrew, nevalah denotes a gross moral abomination — a sexual outrage so severe it violates the covenant community’s identity before YHWH. It implies that the act is ‘not done in Israel’ because it degrades what YHWH has made sacred. The same word is used for the gang-rape at Gibeah, Judg 19:23-24, and for Shechem’s rape of Dinah, Gen 34:7)
innah (to humble/violate — the verb for rape; Amnon ‘humbled’ Tamar, stripping her of honor and status)

ketonet passim (long-sleeved or ornamented robe — often translated ‘coat of many colors’ in the Joseph story, Gen 37:3, but the actual meaning is debated. It was a distinctive garment marking special status: for Joseph it marked his father’s favoritism; for Tamar it marked her as an unmarried princess. When Tamar tears this robe publicly, she is making her violated status visible for all to see — the torn garment is a physical declaration that her protected status has been destroyed)
sin’ah (hatred — Amnon’s post-rape hatred; lust masquerading as love inverts to hatred once the desire is gratified)
gez-hattson (sheep-shearing — the feast occasion Absalom uses to lure Amnon; sheep-shearing was a major social event in Israelite life)
Geshur (the Aramean kingdom where Absalom flees — his mother Maacah was daughter of Talmai, king of Geshur, 2 Sam 3:3)

chapters.title: Absalom’s Rebellion — Exile, Return, Coup, and Death
chapters.synopsis: Joab engineers Absalom’s return from Geshur by sending a ‘wise woman’ of Tekoa to tell David a parable about a mother whose surviving son is threatened with execution for killing his
hebrew_terms: ganav lev (to steal the heart — literally ‘to steal the heart/mind.’ In Hebrew, the ‘heart’ (lev) is not primarily the seat of emotions as in modern English — it is the seat of thought, will, and decision-making. To ‘steal the heart’ is to hijack someone’s judgment and loyalty through deception. Absalom ‘stole the hearts of the men of Israel’ (15:6) by making false promises and positioning himself as a better alternative to David — political seduction masquerading as concern for justice)
etsah (counsel — the contrasting counsels of Ahithophel and Hushai; YHWH defeats the superior counsel to bring judgment on Absalom)
ya’ar Ephrayim (the forest of Ephraim — the battle site east of the Jordan where the trees kill more than the sword, 18:8)
elah (oak/terebinth — the great tree in which Absalom is caught; his famous hair and beauty become his undoing)
beni Avshalom (my son Absalom — David’s grief cry, repeated five times in 18:33; the anguish of a father who loses what the sword has taken)
Ahithophel (David’s counselor turned traitor — likely Bathsheba’s grandfather, whose defection is personal vengeance for David’s sin against his family)

chapters.title: Restoration, Division, and Sheba’s Revolt
chapters.synopsis: David’s grief over Absalom threatens to undo the victory. Joab rebukes the king with brutal honesty: ‘You have today covered with shame the faces of all your servants... for today I know t
hebrew_terms: ish beliyya’al (worthless man/son of Belial — Sheba ben Bichri; this is NOT simply an insult like ‘worthless fellow.’ In Hebrew, beliyya’al literally means ‘without profit’ or ‘without worth,’ but it functions as a category label for someone who acts outside YHWH’s moral order — a person so corrupt they undermine the covenant community itself. In later Second Temple Judaism the term evolved into a proper name for a demonic figure, ‘Belial,’ the spirit of lawlessness. Paul uses it this way in 2 Cor 6:15. The same term is used for the wicked men of Gibeah, Judg 19:22)
chelqah (portion/inheritance — ‘we have no portion in David’; the tribal grievance that will split the kingdom under Rehoboam, 1 Kgs 12:16)
chakham / ishah chakhamah (wise / wise woman — the woman of Abel Beth-maacah who saves her city by negotiating Sheba’s death; wisdom as a feminine virtue in Israel)
neshiqah (kiss — Joab’s treacherous kiss to Amasa before the murder, 20:9; deception through intimacy)
teshuvah (return/restoration — David’s return to Jerusalem; the kingdom is physically restored but spiritually fractured)
chesed (covenant loyalty — David’s mercy to Shimei and his loyalty to Barzillai; grace amid political chaos)

chapters.title: The Gibeonite Atonement — Famine, Broken Covenant, and Rizpah’s Vigil
chapters.synopsis: A three-year famine strikes Israel. David seeks YHWH’s face, and YHWH answers: ‘There is bloodguilt on Saul and on his house, because he put the Gibeonites to death’ (21:1). The Gibe
hebrew_terms: ra’av (famine — the physical consequence of covenant violation; the land itself suffers when sworn oaths are broken before YHWH)
dam / damim (blood / bloodguilt — Saul’s bloodguilt against the Gibeonites persists beyond his death; corporate and transgenerational accountability)
kippur / kaphar (atonement/covering — the Gibeonites require a form of atonement for the covenant violation; the verb is related to the Day of Atonement terminology)
shevu’ah (oath — Joshua’s covenant oath to the Gibeonites in Josh 9:15, 19; YHWH holds Israel accountable for oaths sworn in his name)
hoqa’ (to hang/impale — the form of execution; public exposure as a covenant-enforcement penalty, parallel to Deut 21:22-23)
Rizpah (hot stone/burning coal — Saul’s concubine whose vigil over her sons’ bodies is a powerful image of maternal grief)

chapters.title: Giant-Killing Heroes — David’s Warriors and the Rephaim Descendants
chapters.synopsis: A brief but theologically dense battle catalogue records four engagements with descendants of ‘the giant’ (haraphah, the Rephaim) in the Philistine wars. In the first encounter, David hims
hebrew_terms: haraphah (the giant/the Raphah — the Rephaim ancestor; these are descendants of the giant lineage linked to the Nephilim and Anakim traditions)
ner Yisrael (lamp of Israel — David as the ‘lamp’ of his people; the metaphor for the Davidic line’s continuity and the covenant promise)
kidon / chanit (spear/javelin — the giants’ weapons described with specific weight; echoing Goliath’s armament in 1 Sam 17:7)
manoar (weaver’s beam — the shaft of the giant’s spear is compared to a loom’s beam; identical description to Goliath’s spear in 1 Sam 17:7)

Rephaim (the dead/the giants — this single word carries two distinct meanings in the Hebrew Bible, and both are relevant here. (1) A historical people group: the Rephaim were an ancient race of exceptionally large warriors who inhabited Canaan before Israel’s arrival. King Og of Bashan was the last of their remnant, and his iron bed was 13 feet long (Deut 3:11). They are connected to the Nephilim of Gen 6:4 and the Anakim who terrified Moses’ spies (Num 13:33). (2) The shades of the dead: in other passages (Isa 14:9; 26:14; Ps 88:10), ‘Rephaim’ refers to the ghostly inhabitants of the underworld — the weakened spirits of the deceased. The Ugaritic texts use the cognate rpum for dead warrior-kings feasting in the netherworld. This dual meaning — giant warriors in life, shades in death — suggests a tradition that these beings occupied a liminal space between the human and the supernatural)
esh-ba-etzba (six-fingered — the polydactyl giant of 21:20; physical anomaly marking the Rephaim as outside normal human parameters)

chapters.title: David’s Song of Deliverance — The Divine Warrior Hymn
chapters.synopsis: David’s great victory hymn, preserved also as Psalm 18, is placed near the end of 2 Samuel as his theological summary of a lifetime under YHWH’s protection. The poem is among the most vivi
hebrew_terms: keruv (cherub — YHWH rides on a cherub, 22:11; the cherubim are the throne-bearers of the divine council, the living chariot of YHWH’s presence)
Elyon (Most High — the title of YHWH as supreme over all ‘elohim; ‘the Most High uttered his voice,’ 22:14)
sela / metsudah (rock / fortress — the opening barrage of protection metaphors; YHWH as the unassailable refuge)
qeren yeshu’ah (horn of salvation — the horn symbolizes strength and kingship; YHWH is the ‘horn’ of David’s deliverance)
mashiach / zera (anointed / seed — the final verse combines both: YHWH shows hesed to his mashiach and to his zera forever, linking the covenant to eternity)
mayim rabbim (many waters — the cosmic waters of chaos from which YHWH rescues David; echoing creation mythology where YHWH subdues the waters)
ruach appayv (breath of his nostrils — the divine blast that exposes the foundations of the world; the warrior God’s anger made manifest)

chapters.title: David’s Last Words and the Catalogue of Mighty Men
chapters.synopsis: Chapter 23 opens with David’s ‘last words’ (divrei David ha’acharonim) — a brief but dense prophetic oracle that functions as his theological testament. David identifies
hebrew_terms: ne’um David (oracle of David — the prophetic formula usually reserved for YHWH’s own speech; David claims prophetic authority for his last words)
mashiach Elohei Ya’akov (anointed of the God of Jacob — David’s self-identification linking his kingship to the patriarchal covenant)
ne’im zemirot Yisrael (sweet psalmist of Israel — or ‘the one to whom the songs of Israel are lovely’; David as Israel’s worship leader)

berit olam (everlasting covenant — Hebrew berit means a solemn, binding agreement, far stronger than a modern ‘contract.’ A berit was sealed by blood sacrifice (the Hebrew verb ‘to make a covenant’ is literally ‘to cut a covenant,’ karat berit, because animals were cut in half during the ceremony, Gen 15:9-18). Olam means ‘forever, perpetual, into the unending future.’ A berit olam is therefore an unbreakable, permanent bond ratified by YHWH himself. David uses this term for the Davidic Covenant of 2 Samuel 7 — YHWH’s unconditional promise that David’s royal line will endure forever. ‘Ordered in all things and secure’ (23:5) means every provision is complete and every contingency covered — it cannot fail)
gibborim (mighty men — the elite warriors of David’s army; the Three and the Thirty)
nesekh (libation/drink offering — David pours out the Bethlehem water as a libation to YHWH; the warriors’ blood-risked water becomes a sacrifice)

chapters.title: The Census, the Destroying Angel, and the Threshing Floor of Araunah
chapters.synopsis: The final chapter of 2 Samuel is among the most theologically complex passages in the Old Testament. ‘Again the anger of YHWH was kindled against Israel, and he incited David against them, sayin
hebrew_terms: satan (adversary — this is one of the most misunderstood terms in the Bible. In Hebrew, satan is NOT a proper name — it is a common noun meaning ‘adversary, accuser, prosecutor.’ In Job 1-2 and Zech 3:1-2, the Hebrew reads ha-satan (‘THE adversary’), with the definite article ‘ha-,’ showing it is a TITLE, not a name — you do not put ‘the’ before someone’s name in Hebrew. Ha-satan functions as a member of the divine council who serves as a cosmic prosecutor — like a district attorney in YHWH’s heavenly court, authorized to bring accusations against humans and to test their faithfulness. He operates WITHIN YHWH’s authority, not independently of it. In 1 Chr 21:1, the word appears WITHOUT the article, which is why some translators read it as a proper name (‘Satan’) — but even here, the being acts within YHWH’s sovereign permission, not as an equal adversary. The later Christian concept of Satan as a fallen rebel lord of hell developed gradually during the Second Temple period and is not yet present in most Old Testament uses of the word)

mal’akh hammashchit (the destroying angel — Hebrew mal’akh means ‘messenger’ (the word English Bibles translate as ‘angel’), and mashchit means ‘destroyer.’ This is not a rogue spirit acting on its own but a member of YHWH’s heavenly host carrying out a specific commission from the divine council. The destroying angel operates under YHWH’s direct command — he destroys when told and stops when told (‘It is enough; now stay your hand,’ 24:16). The same type of agent struck Egypt’s firstborn at the Passover (Exod 12:23) and will later annihilate 185,000 Assyrians in a single night (2 Kgs 19:35). In 1 Chr 21:16, this angel is visible, standing between earth and heaven with a drawn sword stretched over Jerusalem)
goren Aravnah (threshing floor of Araunah — the Jebusite threshing floor on Mount Moriah; the future Temple site, connecting David’s sacrifice to Solomon’s Temple and Abraham’s binding of Isaac)

nacham (to relent/be grieved — this Hebrew word creates enormous theological confusion in translation. Older English Bibles translate it as ‘repent’ (‘the LORD repented of the evil’), which misleadingly suggests God made a mistake and changed his mind. The actual meaning is closer to ‘relent’ or ‘feel deep grief.’ When YHWH nacham-s, he does not admit error — he grieves over the suffering his justice requires, and his compassion sets a limit on his judgment. In 24:16, YHWH ‘relented from the calamity’ means his mercy arrested his wrath before it completed its course. The same word is used in Gen 6:6 (‘YHWH was grieved that he had made man’) and Jonah 3:10 (‘God relented from the disaster’). In each case the dynamic is the same: divine justice is real, but divine compassion determines how far it goes)
manah (to number/count — the census; David counts Israel’s military strength, implicitly trusting numbers rather than YHWH)
olah / shelamim (burnt offering / peace offering — the sacrifices David offers on Araunah’s threshing floor to avert the plague; atonement through sacrifice)

chapters.title: Adonijah's Bid and Solomon's Anointing
chapters.synopsis: David is old and feeble. His son Adonijah, the eldest surviving prince, attempts to seize the throne by hosting a sacrificial feast at En-rogel, enlisting Joab the general and Abiathar the priest. But
hebrew_terms: mashach (to anoint -- the act that designates YHWH's chosen king; root of mashiach, 'anointed one,' which becomes 'Messiah' in English via Greek Christos. Every Israelite king was a mashiach, but the term eventually pointed to THE ultimate Anointed One)
shofar (ram's horn trumpet -- the instrument of royal proclamation and divine theophany; its blast marks coronations, holy days, and the coming day of YHWH)
gichon (Gihon spring -- Jerusalem's primary water source; the name echoes the river of Eden, Gen 2:13, connecting the anointing site to paradise geography)
pirdah (mule -- the royal mount; riding the king's mule was a public claim to the throne, an ANE succession convention)
kohen (priest -- Zadok, the faithful priest who anoints Solomon; Abiathar, who backed Adonijah, descends from Eli's condemned line)
navi (prophet -- Nathan the navi orchestrates the succession; the prophet's role in king-making is uniquely Israelite -- no Mesopotamian king required prophetic authorization)

chapters.title: Solomon's Wisdom -- The Gift of a Listening Heart
chapters.synopsis: Solomon goes to Gibeon, the 'great high place' where the tabernacle of meeting still stands, and offers a thousand burnt offerings. That night YHWH appears to Solomon in a dream: 'Ask what I shall giv
hebrew_terms: lev shomea (a listening/understanding heart -- lit. a heart that hears; the organ of discernment)
chokhmat elohim (wisdom of God -- divine wisdom dwelling in a human king)
biyn (to discern/understand -- the ability to distinguish between good and evil)
mishpat (justice/judgment -- the king's primary function: rendering verdicts)
chalom (dream -- the medium of YHWH's revelation to Solomon at Gibeon)
bamah (high place -- Gibeon as the 'great high place' where the tabernacle stood)

chapters.title: The Temple -- Cosmic Mountain, Eden Restored, YHWH's Name Enthroned
chapters.synopsis: Solomon builds the Temple in seven years (6:38), a number that echoes the seven days of creation. The interior is cedar, gold, and cherubim -- Eden imagery saturating every surface. The Holy of Holies
hebrew_terms: bayit YHWH (house of YHWH -- the Temple; in Hebrew bayit means both 'house' and 'palace.' The Temple is simultaneously YHWH's dwelling, his throne room, and his royal palace. ANE temples were always understood as the deity's house -- the place where the god 'lived' and received visitors, offerings, and petitions)

devir (inner sanctuary / Holy of Holies -- the innermost room of the Temple, a perfect 20-cubit cube, where the ark and the cherubim stood. Only the high priest could enter, once per year on the Day of Atonement. This was YHWH's throne room -- the earthly counterpart of the heavenly council chamber)

keruvim (cherubim -- composite winged beings who guard the divine throne; NOT the chubby baby angels of Renaissance art. In ANE iconography, the cherubim are powerful hybrid creatures -- human-faced, winged, often with lion or bull bodies -- who serve as throne guardians. They guarded Eden's entrance (Gen 3:24), formed the mercy seat on the ark (Exod 25:18-22), and in Ezekiel's visions carry YHWH's mobile throne (Ezek 1, 10))

kavod YHWH (glory of YHWH -- Hebrew kavod literally means 'weight, heaviness,' translated 'glory.' It refers to YHWH's visible, radiant, weighty presence -- the luminous cloud-fire manifestation that appeared at Sinai, filled the tabernacle (Exod 40:34), and now fills the Temple. NOTE: Later Jewish tradition developed the term Shekinah (from shakan, 'to dwell') to describe God's indwelling presence. Shekinah does not appear in the Bible itself but became standard rabbinic vocabulary for what 1 Kings 8 describes as the kavod filling the Temple. The kavod and the Shekinah refer to the same reality: God's manifest, localized presence among his people)

anan (cloud -- the theophanic cloud that fills the Temple; the same cloud that appeared at Sinai (Exod 19:16-18) and filled the tabernacle (Exod 40:34-35). In ANE religion, clouds were associated with divine presence and mystery)

shem (name -- YHWH's name 'dwelling' in the Temple; Deuteronomy's Name theology is carefully nuanced: YHWH himself transcends the cosmos, but his Name -- his accessible, revealed identity -- is localized in the Temple. This prevents both the error of thinking God is confined to a building and the error of thinking God is distant and unreachable)
shamayim hashamayim (heaven of heavens / highest heaven -- YHWH's true dwelling; Solomon affirms that even the cosmos cannot contain YHWH, let alone a building)
tephillah (prayer -- the seven-fold dedication prayer; seven petitions for seven situations, the number of creation-completeness)

chapters.title: Queen of Sheba, Solomon's Splendor, and the Apostasy That Breaks a Kingdom
chapters.synopsis: The Queen of Sheba visits Solomon, drawn by his fame, and is overwhelmed: 'The half was not told me. Your wisdom and prosperity surpass the report that I heard' (10:7). She blesses YHWH and recognizes
hebrew_terms: ashtoreth (Ashtoreth/Astarte -- the Sidonian goddess of fertility and war; the West Semitic form of Mesopotamian Ishtar. In the divine council framework, she is one of the elohim allotted to govern Sidon under the Deuteronomy 32:8-9 dispensation)

kemosh (Chemosh -- the national deity of Moab; attested on the Mesha Stele where King Mesha credits Chemosh with military victories. Chemosh received human sacrifice, including Israelite captives dedicated as herem, 'devoted destruction')

milkom (Milcom/Molech -- the Ammonite deity; associated with child sacrifice through fire. The name may derive from melek, 'king,' with the vowels of boshet, 'shame,' substituted by scribes to express revulsion)
shiqquts (detestable thing / abomination -- the strongest Deuteronomic term for foreign gods; literally something that makes one shudder with disgust)

bamah (high place -- an elevated outdoor worship site, usually on a hilltop, with an altar, a sacred tree or pole, and a standing stone. Solomon builds these for foreign gods on the hills east of Jerusalem -- the Mount of Olives, directly across from the Temple)

satan (adversary -- YHWH raises adversaries (satans) against Solomon: Hadad, Rezon, Jeroboam. The Hebrew word satan means 'adversary, accuser' -- here it refers to human political opponents raised by divine decree, the same term later used for the heavenly accuser in Job 1-2)
natah (to turn aside -- Solomon's heart 'turned' after other gods; the same verb of apostasy that Deuteronomy warns against)

davaq (to cling/cleave -- Solomon 'clung' to his foreign wives; this is the same verb used for a man clinging to his wife in Gen 2:24 and for Israel clinging to YHWH in Deut 10:20. Solomon's covenantal devotion is misdirected)

chapters.title: The Kingdom Torn -- Rehoboam, Jeroboam, and the Golden Calves
chapters.synopsis: After Solomon's death, all Israel gathers at Shechem to make Rehoboam king. Jeroboam returns from Egypt and, with the assembly, petitions Rehoboam to lighten the harsh labor Solomon imposed. Rehoboam'
hebrew_terms: eglei zahav (calves of gold -- golden calves; likely bull-pedestals for YHWH's invisible presence, functioning like the cherubim on the ark but without divine authorization. In ANE iconography, deities often stood on the backs of bulls or lions; the pedestal was not the god itself but the god's platform. Jeroboam's calves may have been YHWH-pedestals, but the danger was obvious: the people could not see the invisible God standing on them, so the calves themselves became the focus of worship)
chatah (to sin/miss the mark -- Jeroboam 'caused Israel to sin'; the Deuteronomistic verdict repeated for nearly every northern king)

shevet (tribe -- the political unit that defines the division: ten tribes (shevatim) to Jeroboam, one to Rehoboam. Hebrew shevet means both 'tribe' and 'rod/staff,' connecting tribal identity to authority)
bet-el (house of God -- the ancient patriarchal shrine where Jacob saw the ladder to heaven (Gen 28:19). Jeroboam corrupts this sacred site into a rival cult center, exploiting its venerable history)
dan (judgment -- the northernmost city of Israel; its shrine was the farthest from Jerusalem, an archaeological site that has yielded a high-place platform and a horned altar)

shechem (shoulder/ridge -- the assembly site; ancient covenant-renewal location from Joshua 24. Shechem was also the site of Abimelech's failed kingship (Judg 9), making it an ominous choice for a new political beginning)

kohanim (priests -- Jeroboam appoints non-Levites 'from among all the people,' violating the Torah requirement that only descendants of Levi serve at the altar. This created a priesthood loyal to the king rather than to YHWH)
sevivah (turn of affairs -- lit. a turning; the narrator attributes the political split to YHWH's decree, showing that divine council purposes operate through human folly)

chapters.title: The Man of God from Judah -- Prophetic Confrontation at Bethel
chapters.synopsis: An unnamed 'man of God' (ish ha'elohim) from Judah arrives at Bethel while Jeroboam is burning incense on his new altar. He cries out against the altar by 'the word of YHWH' with a specific prophecy:
hebrew_terms: ish ha'elohim (man of God -- a prophetic title indicating direct divine commission)
davar YHWH (word of YHWH -- the authoritative divine decree the prophet carries)
mofet (sign/portent -- the altar splitting as confirmation of the prophetic word)
navi (prophet -- the old prophet of Bethel who lies to the man of God)
malakh (angel/messenger -- the old prophet falsely claims an angelic command)
aryeh (lion -- the instrument of divine judgment against the disobedient prophet)

chapters.title: The Parade of Judgment -- From Jeroboam to Omri
chapters.synopsis: The narrative accelerates through a succession of kings, each assessed by the Deuteronomistic standard. In the north: Jeroboam's dynasty is condemned through Ahijah's prophecy -- 'I will cut off from
hebrew_terms: ra (evil -- the recurring assessment: 'he did evil (ra) in the eyes of YHWH.' This is the Deuteronomistic History's standard verdict -- every king is measured by covenant faithfulness, not political achievement. Omri was a successful state-builder, but the narrator dismisses him in six verses because he 'did evil')

derekh Yarovam (way of Jeroboam -- the path of cultic innovation that becomes the standard of apostasy for ALL northern kings; the golden calves at Dan and Bethel that opened the door to full idolatry)

asherah (Asherah -- the Canaanite goddess, consort of El in Ugaritic mythology, and the wooden pole or carved image that represented her at worship sites. Asherah poles were set up beside altars; they symbolized fertility and the female divine principle. Israel was repeatedly drawn to Asherah worship alongside YHWH worship, as if YHWH needed a consort -- a category error that imports Canaanite theology into Israelite faith)

baal (Baal -- NOT a single god but a TITLE meaning 'lord' or 'master.' The most prominent Baal was Hadad, the Canaanite storm god. Through Jezebel, Baal Melqart of Tyre is now imported into Israel. This is a qualitative escalation: Jeroboam worshipped YHWH wrongly; Ahab worships a different god entirely)

izevel (Jezebel -- the Sidonian princess, daughter of Ethbaal/Itto-Baal. Josephus identifies Ethbaal as a priest of Astarte who murdered his way to the throne. Jezebel imports not just Baal worship but a Phoenician royal ideology in which the king's will is absolute law -- a direct clash with Israelite Torah-limited kingship)

shomeron (Samaria -- the new northern capital built by Omri on a fortified hill purchased from Shemer for two talents of silver. Archaeologically, Samaria's Israelite remains include fine ashlar masonry and the famous Samaria Ivories -- luxury carvings that confirm the biblical portrait of the Omride dynasty's wealth)

qadesh (cult prostitute -- Hebrew qadesh, from the root q-d-sh meaning 'holy.' These were ritual functionaries dedicated to Canaanite fertility worship -- the bitter irony of 'holiness' language applied to sexual cult practices. Both male and female qedeshim served at high places)

mas (forced labor / tribute -- the corvee labor Solomon imposed and Rehoboam refused to lighten. ANE kings routinely conscripted their subjects for building projects; Israel's Torah placed limits on royal power that Canaanite kingship did not recognize)

elijah.name: Elijah
themes: Contest at Carmel — Baal vs YHWH

chapters.title: Elijah's Appearance -- Drought, Ravens, and the Widow of Zarephath
chapters.synopsis: Elijah the Tishbite appears without introduction: 'As the LORD, the God of Israel, lives, before whom I stand, there shall be neither dew nor rain these years, except by my word' (17:1). The phrase 'b
hebrew_terms: eliyahu (Elijah -- 'My God is YHWH'; the name itself is a polemic against Baal)
amadti lephanav (I stand before him -- divine council throne-room language; a messenger who has access)
orevim (ravens -- unclean birds that feed YHWH's prophet; God overrides purity categories for provision)
tsarephath (Zarephath -- a Sidonian city in Baal's territory; YHWH operates on foreign soil)
kad (jar -- the flour jar that does not empty; miraculous provision in famine)
nephesh (soul/life -- the child's nephesh returns to him when Elijah prays; YHWH controls life and death)

chapters.title: The Contest at Mount Carmel -- Fire from the Divine Council
chapters.synopsis: After three years of drought, YHWH sends Elijah to confront Ahab. Obadiah, the palace steward who hid 100 prophets of YHWH in caves during Jezebel's purge, reluctantly arranges the meeting. Elijah iss
hebrew_terms: se'ippim (divided opinions / crutches -- limping between two positions; the halting of indecision)
esh YHWH (fire of YHWH -- the fire that falls from heaven; the signature of divine council action)
YHWH hu ha'Elohim (YHWH, he is God -- the people's confession; the theological verdict of the contest)
karmel (Carmel -- vineyard/garden of God; the mountain at the boundary of YHWH's and Baal's territory)
hitgoded (to cut/slash oneself -- the ecstatic self-mutilation of Baal's prophets)
olah (burnt offering -- the sacrifice consumed entirely by fire; given wholly to God)

chapters.title: The Still Small Voice -- Elijah at Horeb
chapters.synopsis: Jezebel swears to kill Elijah within 24 hours. The prophet who faced 450 prophets of Baal now flees in terror from one woman. He goes into the wilderness, sits under a broom tree, and asks to die: 'It
hebrew_terms: qol demamah daqqah (sound of thin silence / still small voice -- the paradoxical medium of YHWH's presence)
chorev (Horeb/Sinai -- the mountain of God; the covenant-making mountain where YHWH first revealed himself to Moses)
me'arah (cave -- where Elijah shelters; possibly the 'cleft of the rock' where Moses stood, Exod 33:22)
qana (to be zealous -- Elijah's self-description; the same word used for YHWH's jealousy)
aderet (mantle/cloak -- Elijah wraps his face in it; the prophetic garment that will pass to Elisha)
sherit (remnant -- the 7,000 who have not bowed to Baal; YHWH preserves a faithful core)

chapters.title: Naboth's Vineyard -- Royal Injustice and Prophetic Judgment
chapters.synopsis: Naboth the Jezreelite owns a vineyard adjacent to Ahab's palace. Ahab offers to buy it or trade for a better vineyard. Naboth refuses: 'The LORD forbid that I should give you the inheritance (nachalah
hebrew_terms: nachalah (inheritance -- the inalienable family land grant; Naboth's refusal is Torah obedience, not stubbornness)
kerem (vineyard -- a symbol of Israel itself throughout the prophets: Isa 5:1-7)
beliyyaal (worthless/wicked men -- the false witnesses Jezebel hires; the sons of Belial)
birakh/qillel (to bless/curse -- Naboth is falsely accused of 'cursing' God and king; Hebrew uses 'bless' euphemistically)
dam (blood -- Naboth's blood cries out; the judicial murder demands divine response)
saq (sackcloth -- Ahab's penitential garment when the judgment is pronounced)

chapters.title: Micaiah and the Divine Council -- The Lying Spirit
chapters.synopsis: Ahab of Israel and Jehoshaphat of Judah plan a joint campaign to retake Ramoth-gilead from Syria. Jehoshaphat requests a prophet. Ahab assembles 400 court prophets who unanimously predict victory: 'Go
hebrew_terms: sod YHWH (council of YHWH -- Hebrew sod means 'intimate counsel, secret deliberation, inner circle.' The sod YHWH is the divine throne room where YHWH presides over his council of spiritual beings and decisions about history are made. Jeremiah 23:18 asks: 'For who among them has stood in the sod (council) of YHWH to see and hear his word?' The answer defines true prophecy: only those who have actually stood in the divine council and witnessed its deliberations can speak YHWH's authentic word. Micaiah has stood in the sod; the 400 prophets have not -- this is the fundamental difference between true and false prophecy)

tseva hashamayim (host of heaven -- 'the army of heaven.' These are the spiritual beings who stand at YHWH's right and left in the throne room -- not decorative courtiers but active agents who participate in governance. They are the same beings referred to as 'sons of God' (bene elohim) in Job 1-2 and as 'holy ones' (qedoshim) in Psalm 89:7)

ruach sheqer (lying spirit -- a specific spirit (ruach) authorized by YHWH to be a 'spirit of falsehood' in the mouths of Ahab's prophets. This is NOT Satan -- the text does not identify the spirit. It is a member of the divine council who volunteers for a specific mission and receives YHWH's authorization. The theological implication is startling: YHWH can authorize deception as an instrument of judgment against the wicked)

patah (to entice/deceive -- the mission verb: 'Who will entice (yefatteh) Ahab?' The same root appears in Exodus 22:16 for seduction and in Jeremiah 20:7 where Jeremiah says YHWH 'enticed' (pittitani) him. The word carries the sense of persuasion that leads to ruin)

kiseh (throne -- YHWH's seat of sovereignty from which the council deliberates. Micaiah sees YHWH 'sitting on his throne (kiseh)' -- the same throne Isaiah will see in Isaiah 6:1 and Daniel in Daniel 7:9. The throne is the center of cosmic governance)

navi (prophet -- both Micaiah (the true navi who has access to the divine council) and the 400 (false nevi'im speaking under the lying spirit's influence). The 400 are not conscious charlatans -- they are genuinely deceived by a spirit sent from YHWH's throne room. This makes false prophecy MORE terrifying, not less: the false prophet may be sincere but spiritually deceived)

letummo (in his innocence/simplicity -- the word describing the archer who kills Ahab: he drew his bow 'at random' (letummo). The archer did not know he was shooting at the king. But the divine council directed the arrow. Human innocence and divine sovereignty intersect in this single word)

chapters.title: Ahaziah and the God of Ekron -- Is There No God in Israel?
chapters.synopsis: Ahaziah son of Ahab falls through the lattice of his upper chamber in Samaria and is injured. He sends messengers to inquire of Baal-zebub, the god of Ekron (a Philistine city), whether he will recove
hebrew_terms: baal-zevuv (Baal-zebub -- 'lord of the flies'; likely a Hebrew mockery of Baal-zebul, 'lord of the exalted dwelling')
malakh YHWH (angel of YHWH -- the divine messenger who dispatches Elijah; a divine council operative)
esh (fire -- the fire from heaven that consumes the soldiers; Elijah's signature element)
ish ha'elohim (man of God -- the title the third captain uses for Elijah)
ekron (Ekron -- one of the five Philistine cities; its god consulted instead of YHWH)

chapters.title: Elijah's Ascent and Elisha's Double Portion
chapters.synopsis: YHWH is about to take Elijah up to heaven in a whirlwind (searah). Elijah and Elisha travel from Gilgal to Bethel to Jericho to the Jordan. At each stop, the sons of the prophets confirm: 'Do you know
hebrew_terms: searah (whirlwind/storm -- the vehicle of Elijah's ascent; theophanic transportation)
merkavah esh (chariot of fire -- the divine war chariot; heavenly military transport)
pi shenayim (double portion -- the firstborn's inheritance; Elisha's request for prophetic succession)
aderet (mantle/cloak -- Elijah's prophetic garment that transfers to Elisha; the symbol of office)
avi avi (my father, my father -- Elisha's cry; the prophetic master as spiritual father)
rekhev Yisrael ufarashav (chariots of Israel and its horsemen -- Elijah as Israel's true defense)

chapters.title: Naaman the Syrian -- YHWH Heals Beyond Israel's Borders
chapters.synopsis: Naaman, commander of the Syrian army, is a mighty warrior with leprosy (tsaraath). A captured Israelite servant girl tells Naaman's wife: 'Would that my lord were with the prophet who is in Samaria! H
hebrew_terms: tsaraath (leprosy / skin disease -- the condition that renders Naaman ritually unclean; YHWH alone can heal it)
taval (to dip/immerse -- the seven-fold immersion in the Jordan; later associated with baptism)
rimmon (Rimmon/Hadad -- the Syrian storm god; Naaman will bow in his temple under duress)
adamah (earth/soil -- the Israelite soil Naaman takes home; reflects territorial deity theology)
na'arah (young girl -- the captive Israelite servant who directs Naaman to Elisha)
shalom (peace -- Elisha's farewell to Naaman: 'Go in peace'; acceptance despite imperfect conditions)

chapters.title: The Angelic Army -- Those Who Are with Us
chapters.synopsis: Elisha performs the miracle of the floating axhead for a prophetic guild member, demonstrating YHWH's care for mundane needs. Then the narrative shifts to the Syrian wars. The king of Syria suspects a
hebrew_terms: rekhev esh vesusim (chariots of fire and horses -- the divine council's military forces; the heavenly army visible)
sanverim (blindness/dazzlement -- the supernatural blindness struck on the Syrian army)
paqach (to open -- YHWH 'opens' the servant's eyes to see the spiritual realm)
har (mountain -- the mountain of Dothan filled with the heavenly army; sacred mountain = divine presence)
chayil (army/force -- the Syrian chayil contrasted with the heavenly chayil)

chapters.title: Jehu's Purge -- Blood and Baal Destroyed
chapters.synopsis: Elisha sends a young prophet to anoint Jehu son of Jehoshaphat son of Nimshi as king of Israel, with the commission to 'strike down the house of Ahab your master, that I may avenge on Jezebel the bloo
hebrew_terms: mashach (to anoint -- Jehu is anointed by a prophetic messenger as YHWH's instrument of judgment. The anointing with oil signifies divine selection and empowerment; the mashiach, 'anointed one,' carries YHWH's commission with YHWH's authority)

shig'on (furious driving / madness -- Jehu's reckless chariot speed. The watchman reports: 'the driving is like the driving of Jehu son of Nimshi, for he drives furiously (beshig'on).' The Hebrew word suggests possession or frenzy -- the energy of divine commission expressed as manic intensity)

naqam (vengeance -- YHWH avenges (naqam) the blood of his prophets through Jehu's violence. Biblical vengeance is not personal revenge but the restoration of justice when blood has been wrongfully shed; it is a divine council function, not a human prerogative)

chelqat Navot (the plot of Naboth -- the specific field where Naboth was murdered and where Joram's body is thrown. The divine council's precision is remarkable: the judgment falls at the exact location of the original crime, fulfilling Elijah's word from 1 Kings 21:19)

herem (devoted destruction / the ban -- the total elimination of Baal worship that Jehu carries out, echoing Joshua's herem against Canaanite cities. The Baal temple is demolished and turned into a latrine -- ultimate desecration)

baal (Baal -- the Canaanite-Phoenician storm deity whose worship Jezebel imported. Jehu's purge eliminates the institutional cult of Baal from Israel, but the golden calves of Jeroboam remain -- partial obedience is still disobedience)

chapters.title: Athaliah, Joash, and the Temple Restored
chapters.synopsis: When Athaliah, mother of Ahaziah, learns of her son's death, she seizes the throne of Judah and attempts to exterminate the entire Davidic line. But Jehosheba, Ahaziah's sister, hides the infant Joash
hebrew_terms: berit (covenant -- the three-fold covenant Jehoiada renews: between YHWH and king, between YHWH and people, and between king and people. Hebrew berit means a solemn, binding agreement sealed by oath and often by blood sacrifice. The covenant is the foundation of Israel's entire political theology: the king does not rule by naked power but by covenant obligation to YHWH and to the people)

ataliyahu (Athaliah -- the only queen regnant in Judah's history. She was a daughter of Ahab and likely Jezebel, which makes her the carrier of Baal worship into the southern kingdom. Her attempted extermination of the Davidic seed is the most direct threat to the messianic line in the Old Testament)

nezer (crown/diadem -- the royal crown placed on Joash at his coronation. The nezer is a symbol of the Davidic dynasty's legitimacy and YHWH's covenant promise; its placement on the child king's head is a public declaration that the divine council's decree (2 Sam 7) has not been nullified)

edut (testimony -- the written copy of YHWH's Torah given to the new king at coronation, as prescribed in Deuteronomy 17:18-20. The king receives both the nezer (crown of power) and the edut (standard of accountability) -- authority and law inseparable)

bedek habayit (repair of the house -- lit. 'the breach of the house'; the Temple restoration program Joash initiates. The Temple had been neglected and possibly vandalized by Athaliah's sons (2 Chr 24:7), who broke into it and used its sacred vessels for Baal worship)

aron (chest -- the collection box Jehoiada placed beside the altar at the Temple entrance, with a hole bored in its lid, for receiving contributions toward the Temple repair. This is one of the earliest recorded public fundraising mechanisms)

chapters.title: The Fall of Samaria -- Theological Autopsy of the Northern Kingdom
chapters.synopsis: Hoshea son of Elah, the last king of Israel, withholds tribute from Assyria and conspires with Egypt. Shalmaneser V of Assyria besieges Samaria for three years. The city falls in 722 BC (under Sargon
hebrew_terms: galah (to uncover/exile -- the Hebrew verb means both 'to uncover, strip bare' and 'to go into exile.' The double meaning is theologically loaded: Israel is stripped bare, exposed, and removed from the land that was YHWH's gift. Exile is the ultimate covenant curse, reversing the exodus)

elohim acherim (other gods -- the territorial deities of the nations; the 'sons of God' allotted to govern non-Israelite peoples under the Deuteronomy 32:8-9 dispensation. Israel's fundamental sin is worshipping these subordinate council members instead of YHWH, the Most High who allotted them)

chuqqot haggoyim (customs of the nations -- walking in the religious and cultural practices of peoples allotted to other elohim. This phrase captures the core Deuteronomic concern: Israel was called to be holy (set apart), not to imitate the nations)

matstsevot (standing stones/pillars -- sacred upright stones erected at worship sites. In Canaanite religion these represented the male deity or marked a sacred spot. Israel originally used them legitimately (Jacob at Bethel, Gen 28:18), but they became associated with Baal worship and were banned by Deuteronomy 16:22)
asherim (Asherah poles -- wooden poles or carved images representing the goddess Asherah, set up at high places beside altars. They symbolized the female divine principle and fertility)

ha'avir ba'esh (to pass through fire -- a euphemism for child sacrifice. Children were burned alive as offerings to the deity Molech at a site called Topheth in the Valley of Hinnom. This was the most extreme violation of YHWH's law, which explicitly forbids it in Leviticus 18:21 and Deuteronomy 18:10)

qesem venachash (divination and sorcery -- occult practices forbidden in Deuteronomy 18:10-12. Divination (qesem) involves seeking hidden knowledge through manipulative techniques; sorcery (nachash) involves interpreting omens. Both bypass YHWH's prophetic revelation and consult unauthorized spiritual sources)

gillulim (dung-pellets -- the most contemptuous Hebrew term for idols, used 48 times in Ezekiel. The word appears to derive from galal, 'to roll,' suggesting round lumps of dung. The narrator uses this term (17:12) to express maximum revulsion at Israel's idolatry)

chapters.title: Hezekiah's Reform and Sennacherib's Invasion -- The Angel Destroys 185,000
chapters.synopsis: Hezekiah son of Ahaz is the first reforming king since the northern kingdom's fall. He 'did what was right in the eyes of the LORD, according to all that David his father had done' (18:3). He removes
hebrew_terms: mal'akh YHWH (angel of YHWH -- Hebrew mal'akh simply means 'messenger'; the English word 'angel' comes from the Greek angelos, which also just means 'messenger.' In the Old Testament, the mal'akh YHWH is a unique figure: sometimes he speaks AS YHWH in the first person, sometimes he is distinguished FROM YHWH as a separate being. This ambiguity led later theologians to identify him as the 'second power in heaven' -- a divine being who carries YHWH's full authority and presence. In this passage he is a divine council agent who executes military judgment, destroying 185,000 Assyrians in one night)
Nehushtan (the bronze thing -- Moses' serpent, now an idol; Hezekiah destroys it, demonstrating that even sacred objects can become idols)

Rabshaqeh (chief cupbearer/field commander -- IMPORTANT: this is NOT a personal name, though English Bibles capitalize it like one. Rabshakeh is an Assyrian TITLE, like 'Secretary of State' or 'Field Marshal.' The Akkadian rab shaqe means 'chief cupbearer,' originally the official who tasted the king's wine but by this period a high-ranking military commander and diplomatic envoy. We do not know the personal name of the man who delivered this speech -- the text identifies him only by his rank. Similarly, 'Tartan' (2 Kgs 18:17) is not a name but means 'supreme commander')
tseva'ot (hosts/armies -- YHWH of hosts, the divine title Hezekiah invokes; the LORD of the heavenly armies)
yoshev hakeruvim (enthroned upon the cherubim -- Hezekiah's prayer address; the divine council throne room)
batach (to trust -- the keyword of the Rabshakeh's taunts: 'On what do you trust?'; Hezekiah's trust is in YHWH alone)
ganan (to defend/shield -- 'I will defend this city'; YHWH as Jerusalem's military protector)

chapters.title: Hezekiah's Illness and the Babylonian Embassy -- Seeds of Exile
chapters.synopsis: Hezekiah falls terminally ill, and Isaiah delivers a blunt message: 'Set your house in order, for you shall die; you shall not recover' (20:1). Hezekiah turns his face to the wall and weeps, appealing
hebrew_terms: emet (truth/faithfulness -- Hezekiah claims he walked in 'emet'; yet his display of treasures lacks wisdom)
lev shalem (whole heart -- complete devotion; Hezekiah's sincere but imperfect faith)
beit nekhoto (his treasure house -- the storehouse Hezekiah shows to Babylonian envoys; a fatal indiscretion)
ma'alot (steps/degrees -- the sundial of Ahaz; the shadow retreats ten steps as a sign of divine power over time)
shalom (peace -- Hezekiah's self-interested relief that judgment will come after his lifetime)
Merodakh Bal'adan (Merodach-baladan -- the Babylonian king who sends envoys; historically Marduk-apla-iddina II)

chapters.title: Manasseh and Amon -- The Worst King and the Point of No Return
chapters.synopsis: Manasseh, Hezekiah's son, reigns fifty-five years -- the longest reign of any Judahite king -- and systematically undoes everything his father accomplished. He rebuilds the high places, erects altars
hebrew_terms: tseva hashamayim (host of heaven -- Hebrew tseva means 'army, host, organized company,' and shamayim means 'heaven/sky.' The phrase therefore means 'the army of heaven' or 'the heavenly host.' But what IS this army? It refers to the sun, moon, stars, and planets understood not merely as astronomical objects but as spiritual beings -- members of YHWH's divine council who serve under his authority (much as officers serve under a king). Deuteronomy 4:19 says YHWH 'allotted' these heavenly bodies to the nations as objects of worship, but Israel was forbidden to worship them because Israel belongs directly to YHWH. When Manasseh worships the tseva hashamayim, he is worshipping YHWH's subordinate council members instead of YHWH himself -- like saluting the generals while ignoring the commander-in-chief)

he'evir ba'esh (passed through the fire -- the Hebrew phrase literally means 'caused to pass through the fire,' a euphemism for child sacrifice. The children were offered to a deity called Molech (or Molek), whose name may derive from the Hebrew word melek ('king') with the vowels of boshet ('shame') substituted by later scribes to express revulsion. The practice involved burning children alive at a site called Topheth in the Valley of Hinnom (ge-hinnom) outside Jerusalem. Archaeological sites in Carthage and other Phoenician colonies have uncovered burial precincts (called tophets) containing cremated remains of infants in sacrificial jars, confirming this practice existed in the broader ANE world. YHWH's Torah explicitly forbids it in Lev 18:21 and Deut 18:10)

Asherah (the Canaanite goddess -- Asherah was the chief female deity of the Canaanite pantheon, often understood as the consort (wife) of El, the high god. In Ugaritic mythology she is called 'Lady Athirat of the Sea' and is the mother of the seventy gods. The word 'asherah' in the Bible can refer to (1) the goddess herself, or (2) a wooden pole or carved image that represented her, set up at worship sites. When the Bible says someone 'made an Asherah,' it means they set up such a cult object. Placing her image in YHWH's Temple was the ultimate desecration because it implied YHWH had a goddess-consort -- importing Canaanite theology into YHWH's own throne room)
ov veyid'oni (medium and necromancer -- those who consult the dead; forbidden in Deut 18:10-12)
qav (measuring line -- 'the measuring line of Samaria'; the same standard of judgment applied to Judah)

Baal (lord/master -- NOT a single god but a TITLE meaning 'lord' or 'owner.' The most prominent Baal was Hadad, the Canaanite storm god who controlled rain, thunder, and agricultural fertility. When the Bible condemns 'worship of Baal,' it refers to the Canaanite religious system built around these storm/fertility deities, which often included ritual prostitution and occasionally child sacrifice. Each region had its own local Baal ('Baal of Peor,' 'Baal of Hermon'), which is why the plural 'Baals' (Be'alim) appears frequently. The fundamental conflict of the Old Testament is between YHWH and Baal as rival sources of agricultural blessing and cosmic power)
dam naqi (innocent blood -- Manasseh filled Jerusalem with it; tradition identifies this with the murder of prophets)
pesel ha'Asherah (the carved image of Asherah -- placed in the Temple itself; YHWH's throne room desecrated)

chapters.title: Josiah's Reform and the Book Found -- The Last Great King
chapters.synopsis: Josiah becomes king at eight years old, and 'he did what was right in the eyes of the LORD and walked in all the way of David his father, and he did not turn aside to the right or to the left' (22:2).
hebrew_terms: sefer hattorah (the Book of the Law -- the scroll discovered in the Temple; identified with Deuteronomy or a form of it)
qara (to tear -- Josiah tears his clothes upon hearing the Torah read; genuine repentance and anguish)
rakh levav (tender/penitent heart -- YHWH's description of Josiah; a heart responsive to God's word)
Huldah (the prophetess -- the female prophet consulted by the king's delegation; the authoritative voice of YHWH)

Topheth (the place of burning -- the specific location in the Valley of Hinnom (ge-hinnom) outside Jerusalem where children were sacrificed by fire. The word topheth may derive from a Hebrew root meaning 'to spit' or 'fireplace,' or from an Aramaic word for 'oven.' Josiah defiles it to make it permanently unusable for worship. The Valley of Hinnom (ge-hinnom) later became Jerusalem's garbage dump, where fires burned constantly -- and this physical reality gave rise to the Greek word Gehenna, which Jesus uses in the New Testament for the place of final judgment. Gehenna is NOT the same as Sheol: Sheol is the shadowy realm of all the dead; Gehenna is the place of fiery judgment for the wicked, and the concept developed much later)

bamot (high places -- elevated outdoor worship sites, usually on hilltops, that were the default form of worship throughout Canaan. These were NOT simply 'tall churches.' A typical bamah featured a raised platform or altar, a sacred tree or wooden pole (asherah), and a standing stone (matstsevah). Before the Jerusalem Temple was built, even faithful Israelites worshipped at high places (Samuel offers sacrifice at one in 1 Sam 9:12-14). But after YHWH chose Jerusalem as the sole legitimate worship site, the high places became dangerous because they blurred the line between YHWH worship and Canaanite religion. Worshippers at high places could easily syncretize -- mixing YHWH worship with Baal rituals at the same location. This is why every reforming king is evaluated by whether he removed the bamot)
Pesach (Passover -- the covenant feast Josiah restores; not kept in this manner since the judges)

chapters.title: The Fall of Jerusalem -- Temple Destroyed, Exile Begins
chapters.synopsis: The final chapters of 2 Kings record the rapid collapse of Judah in three stages. First, Jehoiakim rebels against Nebuchadnezzar, and YHWH sends bands of Chaldeans, Syrians, Moabites, and Ammonites ag
hebrew_terms: galut (exile/deportation -- the great exile to Babylon; YHWH's covenant judgment executed)
saraf (to burn -- the Temple, the palace, every great house burned; YHWH's earthly throne room destroyed)
natats (to break down -- the walls of Jerusalem demolished; the city's defenses unmade)
iveir (to blind -- Zedekiah's eyes put out after he watches his sons killed; the last Davidic king to sit on the throne in Jerusalem)
pi-YHWH (at the mouth/command of YHWH -- 'at the command of YHWH'; the exile is not political accident but divine decree)
shulchan (table -- Jehoiachin given a seat at Evil-merodach's table; provision in exile, hope for the Davidic line)
nasa rosh (to lift the head -- Evil-merodach 'lifted the head' of Jehoiachin; a gesture of favor and restoration)

chapters.title: The Two Ways and the Enthroned Son -- Gateway to the Psalter
chapters.synopsis: Psalms 1 and 2 function as a single literary gateway to the entire Psalter. Psalm 1 establishes the two-ways theology: the righteous man who meditates on Torah prospers like a tree planted by streams
hebrew_terms: ashre (blessed/happy -- the opening word of the Psalter; a state of flourishing under God's favor)
meshiach (anointed one -- the king YHWH has installed on Zion; the root of 'Messiah')
ben (son -- the divine decree of sonship over the Davidic king; Ps 2:7, 12)
nachalah (inheritance -- the nations given to the son as his possession)
hagah (to meditate/murmur -- audible recitation of Torah, same verb as Josh 1:8)
choq (decree -- the divine decree of enthronement, the royal protocol from YHWH's council)
regash (to conspire/rage -- the tumultuous rebellion of the nations against YHWH)

chapters.title: What Is Man? -- Human Dominion and the Divine Council
chapters.synopsis: Psalm 8 is the creation psalm of Book I -- a hymn of praise that begins and ends with the majesty of YHWH's name in all the earth. Between the bookends lies the central question of the Psalter and of
hebrew_terms: enosh (man/mortal -- emphasizing human frailty and mortality)
ben-adam (son of man/son of Adam -- the human being in his earthly, creaturely identity)
elohim (gods/heavenly beings -- the divine council members; some translations render 'angels')
me'at (a little -- the narrow gap between humans and the divine council beings)
kavod (glory -- the radiance of the divine presence shared with humanity)
hadar (honor/majesty -- royal splendor conferred on the human vice-regent)
mashal (to rule/have dominion -- the Genesis 1 mandate restated)

chapters.title: Resurrection Hope and the Passion of the Righteous One
chapters.synopsis: Psalms 16 and 22 are the two most significant messianic psalms in Book I -- one pointing to resurrection, the other to the suffering that precedes it. Psalm 16 is a miktam of David expressing unshakab
hebrew_terms: miktam (a psalm type of uncertain meaning -- possibly 'inscription' or 'golden psalm')
chasid (faithful one/holy one -- the one devoted to YHWH who is preserved from Sheol)
shachat (pit/corruption -- the grave; YHWH will not allow his chasid to decay)
eli eli lamah azavtani (my God my God why have you forsaken me -- the opening cry of Ps 22)
kaaru (they pierced -- the disputed reading of Ps 22:16; MT has ka'ari, 'like a lion')
yechalqu (they divide -- casting lots for garments; fulfilled at the cross)
adat mere'im (congregation of evildoers -- the hostile assembly surrounding the sufferer)

chapters.title: The Shepherd and the King of Glory
