drakon megas pyrros (great red dragon -- the chaos serpent of Genesis 3, Isaiah 27:1, and Psalm 74:13-14; the seven heads echo the seven-headed Leviathan of Ugaritic mythology)
ophis ho archaios (the ancient serpent -- ho archaios means 'the original, primordial one'; this IS the nachash of Genesis 3, now identified by his cosmic title)
diabolos kai ho Satanas (the devil and Satan -- diabolos means 'slanderer/accuser' in Greek; satan means 'adversary/accuser' in Hebrew; two names for one being)
ho kategor (the accuser -- the prosecutorial role Satan plays in the divine council; cf. Job 1-2 and Zechariah 3:1, where the satan accuses before YHWH's throne)
Michael (Michael -- 'Who is like God?'; the archangel who fights for Israel; the commander of the loyal divine council forces; cf. Daniel 10:13, 21; 12:1; Jude 9)
haima tou arniou (the blood of the Lamb -- the weapon that defeats the dragon; not military force but sacrificial death; the cross is the cosmic victory)
logos tes martyrias auton (the word of their testimony -- the saints' faithful witness, even unto death; martyria is the root of 'martyr')

chapters.title: The Two Beasts -- The Dragon's Agents: Political Power and Religious Deception
chapters.synopsis: Having lost his place in the divine council (chapter 12), the dragon delegates his authority to two agents: the beast from the sea (thalassa, 13:1) and the beast from the earth (ge, 13:11). Together w
hebrew_terms: therion ek tes thalasses (beast from the sea -- the chaos monster arising from the primordial waters; Daniel 7's four beasts fused into one; political empire animated by the dragon)
therion ek tes ges (beast from the earth -- the false prophet; religious deception that enforces worship of the first beast; the propaganda arm of the dragon's empire)
charagma (mark -- a brand, stamp, or seal; the counterpart of the seal of God (7:3); economic and social control through forced identification with the beast system)
eikona tou theriou (image of the beast -- the idol that the second beast creates and animates; demanded worship; parallels Nebuchadnezzar's golden image in Daniel 3)
hex hex hex / chi xi stigma (666 -- the number of the beast; the gematria value of Neron Qesar in Hebrew; or the number of incomplete humanity: 6-6-6, always falling short of 7)
exousia (authority -- the delegated power the dragon gives to the beast; all earthly authority that demands ultimate allegiance is derivative from the spiritual realm)
blasphemiai (blasphemies -- the beast's speech against God; cf. Daniel 7:25, the little horn 'speaking great things against the Most High')

chapters.title: The Lamb on Zion, Three Angels, and the Harvest of the Earth
chapters.synopsis: Chapter 14 provides a series of vignettes that preview the outcome of the cosmic war. First, the Lamb stands on Mount Zion with the 144,000, who bear his name and his Father's name on their foreheads
hebrew_terms: Sion (Zion -- the holy mountain; the Lamb's counter-position to the dragon's throne; the true seat of cosmic authority)
euangelion aionion (eternal gospel -- the timeless proclamation that God is Creator, Judge, and King; not a 'new' gospel but the original and permanent one)
drepanon (sickle -- the instrument of harvest; the eschatological reaping of the earth; cf. Joel 3:13, 'Put in the sickle, for the harvest is ripe')
lenos tou thymou tou theou (winepress of the wrath of God -- the judgment imagery from Isaiah 63:1-6, where YHWH treads the winepress and his garments are stained with 'blood')
aparchai (firstfruits -- the 144,000 as firstfruits to God and the Lamb; the first installment of the full harvest; cf. 1 Corinthians 15:20, Christ the firstfruits)

chapters.title: The Seven Bowls of Wrath -- The Final Plagues and the Song of Moses
chapters.synopsis: Chapters 15-16 present the third and final cycle of divine judgment: the seven bowls (phialai) of God's wrath, introduced by a heavenly scene of worship (15:1-8) and executed in rapid succession (16:1
hebrew_terms: phiale (bowl -- a broad, shallow libation bowl used in temple worship; the imagery of worship vessels turned into instruments of judgment)
ode Mouseos kai ode tou arniou (song of Moses and song of the Lamb -- the Exodus liberation song combined with the Lamb's victory song; old covenant and new covenant united)
Harmageddon / Armageddon (Hebrew: Har Megiddo, 'Mount Megiddo' -- the eschatological battlefield; Megiddo was the site of many decisive battles in Israel's history)
Gegonen (It is done! -- the divine declaration of completion; cf. John 19:30, tetelestai, 'It is finished'; the wrath is completed, the verdict fully executed)
naos (temple/inner sanctuary -- the heavenly temple opened to reveal the bowls; the dwelling of God from which judgment proceeds)
epta plegas tas eschatas (seven last plagues -- the final, complete set of divine judgments; after these, God's wrath is finished)

chapters.title: The Fall of Babylon -- The Whore, the Beast, and the Lament of the World
chapters.synopsis: Chapters 17-18 provide the detailed account of Babylon's destruction, previewed in 14:8 and enacted by the seventh bowl in 16:19. One of the bowl angels shows John 'the great prostitute (pornes tes me
hebrew_terms: porne megale (great prostitute -- the world-system that seduces the nations into idolatry; the OT metaphor of unfaithfulness as sexual immorality; cf. Hosea 1-3; Ezekiel 16; 23)
Babylon he megale (Babylon the great -- the cumulative symbol of every empire that opposes God; historical Babylon, Rome, and every system that demands ultimate allegiance)
meter ton pornon (mother of prostitutes -- the source and paradigm of all false worship; the Babel spirit that generates every subsequent idolatrous civilization)
mysterion (mystery -- written on her forehead; the hidden spiritual reality behind the visible city/empire system; the unseen realm's corruption made visible)
hepta ore (seven mountains -- Rome's seven hills; the Septicollis, the defining geographic feature of the Roman capital; but also symbolic of comprehensive earthly power)
mylos (millstone -- the instrument of Babylon's irreversible destruction; Jesus used the same image for those who cause little ones to stumble, Mark 9:42)

chapters.title: The Hallelujah Chorus and the Divine Warrior -- The Marriage Supper and the Final Battle
chapters.synopsis: Chapter 19 divides into two halves: the heavenly celebration after Babylon's fall (19:1-10) and the divine warrior's final triumph (19:11-21). The celebration begins with the only occurrence of 'Halle
hebrew_terms: Hallouia (Hallelujah -- Hebrew: hallelu-Yah, 'Praise YHWH!'; the only NT occurrence; the culminating praise of the entire Bible, echoing the Hallel psalms 113-118 and 146-150)
gamos tou arniou (marriage of the Lamb -- the covenant union between Christ and his church; the eschatological fulfillment of the prophetic marriage metaphor of Hosea, Isaiah 54, and Ephesians 5)
ho Logos tou Theou (The Word of God -- Christ's name as divine warrior; the creative and judicial word of God personified; cf. John 1:1, 'the Word was God')
rhomphaia oxeia (sharp sword -- the word of judgment from Christ's mouth; not a physical weapon but the divine decree that conquers; cf. Isaiah 49:2; Hebrews 4:12)
limne tou pyros (lake of fire -- the final destination of the beast, false prophet, and eventually death and Hades; the 'second death'; not annihilation but eternal judgment)
diademata polla (many diadems -- royal crowns, not victory wreaths [stephanoi]; Christ wears the crowns that rightfully belong to him, unlike the dragon's seven diadems [12:3])

chapters.title: The Millennium, Satan's Final Defeat, and the Great White Throne -- The Last Judgment
chapters.synopsis: Revelation 20 is the most debated chapter in the book. An angel binds Satan with a chain and throws him into the abyss for a thousand years (chilia ete, 20:1-3). The saints who remained faithful and t
hebrew_terms: chilia ete (a thousand years -- the 'millennium'; whether literal or symbolic is the central debate; 10 x 10 x 10 = a comprehensive, complete period of divine rule)
anastasis he prote (the first resurrection -- the resurrection of the faithful who reign with Christ; the 'second death has no power over them' [20:6])
Gog kai Magog (Gog and Magog -- from Ezekiel 38-39; the eschatological enemies who attack God's people; in Revelation, they are gathered by Satan for the final rebellion)
thronos megas leukos (great white throne -- the ultimate judicial seat; white = purity and holiness; 'great' = the supreme court of the cosmos)
biblia / biblos tes zoes (books / book of life -- the record of deeds and the register of the redeemed; cf. Daniel 7:10; Exodus 32:32-33; Psalm 69:28; Philippians 4:3)
thanatos ho deuteros (the second death -- the lake of fire; not physical death but eternal separation from God; the ultimate consequence for those not in the book of life)
abyssos (the abyss -- the prison of fallen spiritual beings; the temporary holding place for Satan during the millennium; cf. 1 Enoch 10:4-6; 2 Peter 2:4; Jude 6)

chapters.title: The New Heaven and New Earth -- The Garden-City-Temple and the Beatific Vision
chapters.synopsis: The final two chapters of Revelation -- and of the entire Bible -- present the culmination of all things: the new creation. 'Then I saw a new heaven and a new earth (ouranon kainon kai gen kainen), fo
hebrew_terms: ouranon kainon kai gen kainen (new heaven and new earth -- kainos means 'new in quality/nature,' not neos, 'new in time'; renewed creation, not replacement)
Ierousalem kaine (New Jerusalem -- the city that descends from heaven; the bride of the Lamb; the garden-city-temple that fulfills and surpasses Eden)
skene tou theou (dwelling place/tabernacle of God -- God's tent pitched among humanity; the wilderness tabernacle made permanent and universal)
kaina poio panta (I am making all things new -- the present tense: the renewal is ongoing and comprehensive; not creating from scratch but transforming what exists)
to xylon tes zoes (the tree of life -- from Genesis 2:9; 3:22-24; barred by the cherubim after the fall, now restored and accessible in the new creation; its leaves heal the nations)
potamos hydatos zoes (river of the water of life -- from Genesis 2:10 and Ezekiel 47:1-12; the life-giving stream that flows from God's presence)
katathema ouk estai eti (no longer any curse -- Genesis 3:14-19 reversed; the ground curse, the pain of childbirth, the thorns and thistles, death itself -- all removed)
opsontai to prosopon autou (they will see his face -- the beatific vision; what Moses was denied [Exod 33:20, 'you cannot see my face and live'] is granted to all the redeemed)
Amen erchou Kyrie Iesou (Amen, come, Lord Jesus -- the final prayer of the Bible; the Aramaic Maranatha of 1 Cor 16:22 rendered in Greek)

chapters.title: Blessed in the Heavenly Places -- The Cosmic Blessing and Christ's Enthronement
chapters.synopsis: Ephesians opens with the most sustained blessing (berakah/eulogia) in the New Testament -- a single Greek sentence of 202 words (1:3-14) that cascades from election before the foundation of the world
hebrew_terms: eulogetos (blessed -- the opening word, echoing the Jewish berakah prayer formula; God is blessed because he has blessed us)
proorizein (to predestine -- literally 'to mark out beforehand'; God's predetermined plan, not arbitrary fate)
mysterion (mystery -- the hidden plan now revealed; not unknowable but previously unrevealed)
anakephalaiosis (summing up/re-heading -- 1:10; God's plan to unite all things under one head, Christ)
en tois epouraniois (in the heavenly places -- the spiritual dimension where blessings reside, Christ is enthroned, and powers operate)
arche (rule/principality -- the highest tier of spiritual powers in the council hierarchy)
exousia (authority -- delegated spiritual power; the second tier of the cosmic hierarchy)
dynamis (power -- inherent spiritual might; a category of spiritual being)
kyriotes (dominion/lordship -- a ruling class of spiritual beings; cf. Col 1:16; Jude 8)
pleroma (fullness -- the totality of divine presence; the church as Christ's pleroma)
arrabon (guarantee/down payment -- the Spirit as the first installment of the coming inheritance)

chapters.title: Dead, Raised, Seated -- From the Domain of the Archon to the Heavenly Places
chapters.synopsis: Ephesians 2 is the chapter of cosmic transfer. In 2:1-10, Paul describes the individual believer's journey from spiritual death under the archon of the air to resurrection and enthronement with Christ
hebrew_terms: archon (ruler/prince -- the 'prince of the power of the air'; Satan as territorial ruler over the present age)
exousia tou aeros (authority of the air -- the atmospheric realm between earth and heaven; the zone of demonic activity in Second Temple cosmology)
aion (age/world-system -- 'the course of this world'; not just time but the entire system of the present evil age)
sarx (flesh -- the sphere of human existence in its weakness and rebellion; not the physical body per se but the orientation toward self and sin)
charis (grace -- unmerited divine favor; the sole cause of salvation, 2:5, 8)
ergon (work -- human deeds; excluded as the basis of salvation, 2:9; but believers are God's workmanship CREATED FOR good works, 2:10)
mesotoichon tou phragmou (the dividing wall of the fence/hostility -- the barrier between Jew and Gentile; likely alluding to the Temple's Court of the Gentiles boundary wall)
kainos anthropos (new man/new humanity -- not neos [renewed old thing] but kainos [qualitatively new]; the single new humanity replacing the Jew-Gentile divide)
akrogoniaios (cornerstone -- Christ as the foundation stone on which the cosmic temple is built; echoes Isa 28:16)
naos (temple/sanctuary -- the inner temple where God dwells; the church as the new temple indwelt by the Spirit)

chapters.title: The Mystery Revealed to the Powers -- The Church's Cosmic Mission
chapters.synopsis: Ephesians 3 contains the letter's theological climax: the revelation of the 'mystery' (mysterion) hidden for ages and now disclosed -- that the Gentiles are fellow heirs with Israel in one body (3:6).
hebrew_terms: mysterion (mystery -- the previously hidden plan now revealed; not unknowable but newly disclosed by the Spirit)
oikonomia (plan/stewardship/administration -- God's household management of history; Paul's stewardship of the mystery)
synkleronoma (fellow heirs -- Gentiles inheriting alongside Israel; syn- prefix = 'together with')
syssoma (fellow members of the body -- Gentiles as co-members of Christ's body; hapax legomenon, only here in the NT)
symmetocha (fellow partakers -- Gentiles sharing in the promise; the triple syn- in 3:6 is emphatic)
polypoikilos (manifold/many-colored -- the dazzling complexity of God's wisdom displayed to the powers)
archai kai exousiai (rulers and authorities -- the spiritual powers in the heavenly places who observe the church)
prothesis ton aionon (the eternal purpose -- literally 'the purpose of the ages'; God's plan spanning all of history)
parresia (boldness -- confident access to God; the opposite of servile fear before the powers)
exischysete (may have strength -- a rare verb; the spiritual capacity to comprehend the incomprehensible love of Christ)

chapters.title: Walk Worthy -- The New Humanity and the Descent of the Victor
chapters.synopsis: The letter pivots from doctrine (chapters 1-3) to practice (chapters 4-6) with the exhortation to 'walk worthy of the calling to which you have been called' (4:1). Paul grounds ecclesial ethics in cos
hebrew_terms: axios (worthy -- to walk in a manner consistent with one's calling; behavior matching identity)
henotes (unity -- the oneness of the body; maintained, not created, by believers; grounded in the seven 'ones' of 4:4-6)
katabainein/anabainein (to descend/to ascend -- the christological descent-ascent pattern; Christ descends to earth/Hades and ascends above all heavens)
aichmalosia (captivity -- 'he led captivity captive'; the conquered enemies paraded in Christ's triumphal procession)
domata (gifts -- from Ps 68:18; the gifts Christ distributes to the church after his ascension; the Spirit's charismatic empowerment)
katartismos (equipping/perfecting -- the purpose of the gifted leaders: equipping the saints for their ministry)
palaios anthropos (old man/old self -- the former humanity under the archon; to be 'put off' like soiled clothing)
kainos anthropos (new man/new self -- the new humanity in Christ; to be 'put on'; created in God's likeness in righteousness and holiness)
photismos (illumination -- walking as 'children of light'; the moral transformation from darkness to light)
plerosthe en pneumati (be filled with the Spirit -- the contrasting imperative to wine-filling; continuous, Spirit-directed worship)

chapters.title: The Cosmic Household and the Armor of God -- Spiritual Warfare in the Heavenly Places
chapters.synopsis: The letter's final section moves from the household code (5:22-6:9) to the climactic spiritual warfare passage (6:10-20). The household code transforms Greco-Roman social conventions by grounding them
hebrew_terms: kephale (head -- Christ as head of the church; the husband as head of the wife; headship as source/authority, debated in scholarship)
mysterion mega (great mystery -- 5:32; the marriage relationship as a window into Christ's love for the church; the cosmic mystery applied to human relationships)
panoplia (whole armor/full armor -- 6:11, 13; the complete equipment of the divine warrior; technical Roman military term for full infantry kit)
pale (wrestling -- 6:12; hand-to-hand combat, not distant warfare; the spiritual battle is intimate and personal)
kosmokrator (cosmic ruler/world-ruler -- 6:12; planetary deities in Greco-Roman astrology; hostile spiritual powers governing human fate in the present darkness)
ta pneumatika tes ponerias (the spiritual forces of evil -- 6:12; literally 'the spiritual things of wickedness'; evil as organized, structured, hierarchical)
skotos (darkness -- the domain of the hostile powers; 'this present darkness' as the current age under the archon's influence)
machaira tou pneumatos (sword of the Spirit -- 6:17; the short Roman thrusting sword, the one offensive weapon; identified with the rhema theou, the spoken word of God)
proseuchomai (to pray -- 6:18; prayer as the sustaining atmosphere of spiritual warfare; 'praying at all times in the Spirit')
tychikos (Tychicus -- 6:21; Paul's trusted messenger, also named in Col 4:7; the letter carrier who connects Paul to the churches)

eph_warfare.name: The Heavenly Places and Spiritual Warfare

chapters.title: Seated Far Above All Rule and Authority -- The Enthronement and the Archon
chapters.synopsis: The 'en tois epouraniois' motif that structures the entire letter begins with Christ's enthronement far above the cosmic hierarchy. Paul prays that believers would comprehend the immeasurable power th
hebrew_terms: en tois epouraniois (in the heavenly places -- the five-fold phrase unique to Ephesians; the spiritual dimension where blessings, Christ's throne, the powers, and spiritual warfare all intersect)
hyperano (far above -- a compound preposition intensifying 'above'; Christ's position is not one rank higher but transcendently superior to every tier of the cosmic hierarchy)
arche (rule/principality -- the highest tier of spiritual authority in the divine council; ruling princes with jurisdiction over cosmic domains)
exousia (authority -- delegated spiritual jurisdiction; the second tier of the cosmic hierarchy; the same word used of Satan's domain in 2:2)
dynamis (power -- inherent spiritual might; beings whose nature is power itself; the third tier in the cosmic order)
kyriotes (dominion/lordship -- the fourth category of spiritual beings, those who exercise lordship; cf. Col 1:16, Jude 8, 2 Pet 2:10)

archon tes exousias tou aeros (the prince of the authority of the air -- Satan's title as territorial ruler over the atmospheric realm between earth and heaven; a rebellious divine council member exercising delegated jurisdiction)
synekathisen (seated together with -- the aorist tense indicates completed action; believers are already enthroned with Christ as a present reality, not a future hope)
energountos (working/operating -- present participle; the archon's power is currently active in those who refuse obedience to God)
to hyperballon megethos (the surpassing greatness -- stacked superlatives describing the power that raised Christ and seats believers; the same resurrection power operates in the church)

chapters.title: The Wisdom of God Made Known to the Powers -- The Church's Cosmic Mission
chapters.synopsis: Ephesians 3:10 is the single most important verse for understanding the church's cosmic identity: 'so that through the church the manifold wisdom (polypoikilos sophia) of God might now be made known t
hebrew_terms: polypoikilos (manifold/many-splendored -- used of intricate embroidery or a tapestry of many colors; the dazzling complexity of God's wisdom revealed through the church to the watching powers)
mysterion (mystery -- the previously hidden plan now apocalyptically disclosed; not unknowable but previously unrevealed, concealed in God since before creation)
oikonomia (stewardship/administration -- God's household management of the cosmic plan; Paul's delegated role in administering the mystery to the Gentiles)
synkleronoma (fellow heirs -- the first of three syn- compounds in 3:6; Gentiles inheriting jointly with Israel, not as secondary beneficiaries but as full co-heirs)
syssoma (fellow members of the body -- a hapax legomenon, coined by Paul for this moment; Gentiles as co-constitutive members of Christ's body, not appendages)
symmetocha (fellow partakers -- the third syn- compound; Gentiles sharing in the promise alongside Israel; the Deuteronomy 32 division reversed in practice)
gnorizo (to make known -- 3:10; the verb of cosmic revelation; the powers are learning God's wisdom through the church's existence)
archai kai exousiai en tois epouraniois (rulers and authorities in the heavenly places -- the audience of the church's cosmic display; both faithful and rebellious members of the divine council)
prothesis ton aionon (the eternal purpose -- literally 'the purpose of the ages'; God's pre-creation plan realized in Christ and displayed through the church)
patria (family/clan -- 3:15; 'from whom every family in heaven and on earth is named'; God's two-household framework, the angelic families in heaven and the human family in the church)

chapters.title: The Full Armor of God Against Cosmic Powers -- Warfare in the Heavenly Places
chapters.synopsis: The letter's final section brings the 'en tois epouraniois' motif to its climax in direct spiritual combat. Paul identifies the enemy with unparalleled specificity: 'We do not wrestle (pale) against f
hebrew_terms: kosmokratores tou skotous (cosmic powers of this darkness -- a term from Greco-Roman astral religion for planetary deities; Paul co-opts it for the hostile spiritual powers whose domain is the darkness of the present age)
pale (wrestling -- 6:12; hand-to-hand combat, the closest and most personal form of ancient warfare; spiritual conflict is not fought at a distance but in direct engagement)
panoplia tou theou (the full armor of God -- 6:11, 13; the complete set of military equipment; not the believer's armor but GOD's own armor, the same gear YHWH wears as warrior in Isaiah 59:17)
machaira tou pneumatos (the sword of the Spirit -- the only offensive weapon in the armor list; identified as 'the word (rhema) of God'; the spoken, active, applied word)
rhema theou (the word of God -- 6:17; not logos [the whole message] but rhema [a specific utterance]; the particular word of God applied to a particular situation in the power of the Spirit)
ta pneumatika tes ponerias (the spiritual forces of evil -- literally 'the spirituals of wickedness'; spiritual beings whose nature is wickedness, operating in the heavenly places)
aletheia (truth -- the belt that holds all the armor together; not merely intellectual truth but the reality of God as opposed to the deceptions of the powers)
dikaiosyne (righteousness -- the breastplate protecting the vital organs; the righteousness of God imputed to believers and expressed in righteous living)

thureos tes pisteos (the shield of faith -- the thureos is the large Roman shield [scutum] covering the whole body, not the small round aspis; faith that extinguishes all the flaming arrows of the evil one)
proseuche kai deesis (prayer and supplication -- 6:18; the atmosphere of spiritual warfare; not one armor piece among many but the means by which all the armor is activated and maintained)

chapters.title: The Gospel of God and the Nations Given Over
chapters.synopsis: Romans opens with Paul's self-introduction as 'a servant of Christ Jesus, called to be an apostle, set apart for the gospel of God' (1:1). The gospel is defined christologically: concerning God's Son,
hebrew_terms: euangelion (gospel/good news -- not just 'salvation information' but the royal proclamation of the king's victory; cf. Isa 52:7)
dikaiosyne theou (the righteousness of God -- God's character, gift, and covenant faithfulness revealed in the gospel)
ek pisteos eis pistin (from faith to/for faith -- the entire dynamic of salvation is faith: originating in God's faithfulness, received by human faith)
apokalyptetai (is revealed -- present tense; the righteousness of God is being continuously unveiled in the gospel proclamation)
orge theou (the wrath of God -- not emotional rage but judicial response to sin; God's settled opposition to evil)
paredoken (gave over/handed over -- the divine judicial abandonment; God gave the nations over to the consequences of their rebellion)
asebeia (ungodliness -- the vertical dimension: failure to worship the Creator)
adikia (unrighteousness -- the horizontal dimension: failure to do justice among humans)
aletheia (truth -- what the nations suppress; the revelation of God available in creation)
eikona (image/likeness -- 1:23; they exchanged the glory of God for images; the reversal of the imago Dei)

chapters.title: The Impartial Judge -- Jews and Gentiles Under Judgment
chapters.synopsis: Having indicted the Gentile world (1:18-32), Paul turns the indictment on the Jewish interlocutor: 'You have no excuse, O man, every one of you who judges. For in passing judgment on another you conde
hebrew_terms: prosopolempsia (partiality/favoritism -- literally 'face-receiving'; 2:11; God shows no favoritism based on ethnic identity)
peritome (circumcision -- the covenant sign of Genesis 17; Paul redefines it as 'of the heart' in 2:29)
nomos (law/Torah -- the Mosaic covenant; possessing it does not guarantee righteousness; it must be DONE)
logia tou theou (the oracles of God -- 3:2; Israel's great privilege: the divine revelation entrusted to them)
pistin tou theou (the faithfulness of God -- 3:3; God's covenant loyalty that is not nullified by human unfaithfulness)
hypodikos (accountable/liable to judgment -- 3:19; a legal term: the whole world stands as a defendant before the divine judge)
ergon nomou (works of the law -- 3:20; deeds performed in obedience to Torah; insufficient for justification)
epignosis hamartias (knowledge of sin -- 3:20; the law's function: not to save but to reveal the depth of the problem)
krima tou theou (the judgment of God -- 2:2; the impartial, righteous verdict that falls on Jew and Gentile alike)
syneidesis (conscience -- 2:15; the moral awareness that functions as an internal law even for those without Torah)

chapters.title: The Righteousness of God Revealed -- Justification by Faith and Abraham's Example
chapters.synopsis: Having established universal guilt (1:18-3:20), Paul now reveals the solution: 'But now (nyni de) the righteousness of God has been manifested apart from the law' (3:21). The 'but now' marks the cosmi
hebrew_terms: nyni de (but now -- the cosmic turning point; the transition from universal condemnation to universal offer of grace)
dikaioumenoi (being justified -- present passive: justification as God's ongoing act of declaring righteous, not human achievement)
dorean (freely/as a gift -- justification is gratis, without cost to the recipient; the same word used for 'for nothing' in secular Greek)
apolytrosis (redemption -- the price paid to liberate a slave or prisoner; Christ's death as the ransom that frees humanity from sin's slavery)
hilasterion (propitiation/mercy seat -- the kapporet of Yom Kippur; Christ as the place where God's wrath is satisfied and mercy is dispensed)
paresis (passing over -- 3:25; God's temporary overlooking of sins in the OT era, now addressed definitively in Christ's death)
logizomai (to reckon/count/credit -- 4:3; the accounting metaphor: faith is 'credited' to the believer's account as righteousness)
adikos (ungodly -- 4:5; God 'justifies the ungodly'; the scandalous claim that God declares the unrighteous righteous through faith)
kleronomos kosmou (heir of the world -- 4:13; Abraham's inheritance is not just Canaan but the entire cosmos; the Abrahamic promise in cosmic scope)
eis to einai auton patera (so that he might be the father -- 4:11; Abraham's identity as patriarch of all believers, Jew and Gentile)

chapters.title: Peace with God and the Adam-Christ Typology -- One Man's Obedience
chapters.synopsis: Chapter 5 is the theological bridge between justification (chapters 1-4) and sanctification (chapters 6-8). In 5:1-11, Paul describes the present blessings of justification: peace with God, access to
hebrew_terms: eirene (peace -- 5:1; not merely absence of conflict but the positive state of restored relationship with God; the shalom of the covenant)
prosagoge (access -- 5:2; the right to enter the king's presence; a court term for introduction to royalty; believers have permanent access to grace)
kauchaomai (to boast/exult -- 5:2, 3, 11; not proud arrogance but confident, joyful celebration of God's work)
thlipsis (tribulation/affliction -- 5:3; suffering that produces endurance, character, and hope; the refining process)
agape (love -- 5:5, 8; God's love poured out through the Spirit; demonstrated in Christ dying for the ungodly)
typos (type/pattern -- 5:14; Adam as a type (prefigurement) of Christ; the first man points forward to the last man)
paraptoma (trespass/transgression -- 5:15-20; a 'falling beside,' a deviation from the path; Adam's sin that brought death to all)
charisma (free gift/grace-gift -- 5:15-16; the gift that overflows from the one man Jesus Christ; grace that exceeds the trespass)
kathistemi (to appoint/constitute -- 5:19; 'the many were made sinners' and 'will be made righteous'; constituted in a new status through representative action)
hyperperisseuo (to super-abound -- 5:20; 'where sin increased, grace abounded all the more'; grace not merely matching sin but exceeding it immeasurably)

chapters.title: Dead to Sin, Alive to God -- Baptismal Union with Christ
chapters.synopsis: Romans 6 addresses the logical objection to justification by grace: 'Are we to continue in sin that grace may abound?' (6:1). Paul's response is baptismal theology: believers have been 'baptized into
hebrew_terms: baptizein eis (to baptize into -- immersion into Christ's death; baptism as participation in the crucifixion event, not merely a symbol)
palaios anthropos (old man/old self -- the Adamic self, the person under sin's dominion; crucified with Christ so it no longer rules)
soma tes hamartias (body of sin -- not the physical body but the entire self as enslaved to sin; the body as sin's instrument, now rendered powerless)
katargeo (to render inoperative/abolish -- 6:6; the 'body of sin' is not destroyed but made powerless, put out of commission)
logizomai (to reckon/consider -- 6:11; the same accounting word from ch. 4; believers must reckon as true what is true: dead to sin, alive to God)
hopla (weapons/instruments -- 6:13; the members of the body as tools available for either sin or righteousness; a military metaphor)
doulos (slave/servant -- 6:16-22; the controlling metaphor: everyone serves something; the question is which master)
opsonia (wages -- 6:23; a military term for soldier's pay; sin pays death as earned wages; God gives life as an unearned gift)
charisma (free gift -- 6:23; contrasted with wages; eternal life is not earned but given; the same root as charis, grace)
kyrieuein (to lord over/dominate -- 6:9, 14; sin and death 'lord it over' the unregenerate; sin will NOT lord over those under grace)

chapters.title: The Law, the Flesh, and the Wretched Man -- The Anguish of the Divided Self
chapters.synopsis: Romans 7 is the most psychologically intense chapter in Paul's letters and one of the most debated in the entire Bible. Paul uses a marriage analogy to show that death releases from legal obligation (
hebrew_terms: nomos (law -- used in multiple senses throughout ch. 7: the Mosaic Torah, the principle of sin, the inner moral law; context determines meaning)
thanatoo (to put to death -- 7:4; believers have been 'put to death' to the law through Christ's body; death ends the law's jurisdiction)
entole (commandment -- 7:8-13; specifically the tenth commandment ('you shall not covet'); the commandment that exposed the heart's deepest rebellion)
epithymia (covetousness/desire -- 7:7-8; the forbidden longing; sin used the commandment to produce 'every kind of desire'; the internal spring of rebellion)
aphorme (opportunity/base of operations -- 7:8, 11; a military term: sin found a 'beachhead' through the commandment; the law became sin's staging ground)
sarx (flesh -- 7:14, 18, 25; the human self in its weakness and opposition to God; 'I am of the flesh, sold under sin')
nous (mind -- 7:23, 25; the rational, willing self that approves God's law but cannot execute it; the mind serves God's law while the flesh serves sin's law)
melos (members -- 7:23; the parts of the body as the theater of the sin-war; 'another law in my members waging war against the law of my mind')
talaiporos (wretched -- 7:24; miserable, afflicted, pitiable; the cry of one who sees the good but cannot reach it)
soma tou thanatou (body of death -- 7:24; the physical existence as the domain of death's power; the mortality that imprisons the willing self)

chapters.title: No Condemnation, No Separation -- The Spirit, Creation's Groaning, and the Invincible Love
chapters.synopsis: Romans 8 is the summit of Pauline theology -- 'the most important chapter in the Bible' (N. T. Wright). It opens with 'no condemnation' (8:1) and closes with 'no separation' (8:39), forming a bracket
hebrew_terms: katakrima (condemnation -- 8:1; the judicial verdict of guilty; no condemnation for those in Christ; the verdict of 5:16, 18 overturned)
nomos tou pneumatos tes zoes (the law of the Spirit of life -- 8:2; the new operating principle: the Spirit's life-giving power that replaces the law of sin and death)
phronema tes sarkos / tou pneumatos (the mind-set of the flesh / of the Spirit -- 8:5-6; two fundamental orientations: death or life-and-peace)
hyiothesia (adoption -- 8:15, 23; the Roman legal act of adoption conferring full inheritance rights and a new family identity; the Spirit's witness to believers' filial status)
Abba ho pater (Abba, Father -- 8:15; the Aramaic intimate address + the Greek; the Spirit enables the cry of the adopted child)
synkleronomoi Christou (co-heirs with Christ -- 8:17; sharing the inheritance of the one who is enthroned above all powers)
apokaradokia tes ktiseos (the eager expectation of creation -- 8:19; literally 'craning the neck'; the entire cosmos straining forward to see the sons of God revealed)
mataiotes (futility/vanity -- 8:20; the purposelessness to which creation was subjected; the cosmos under the curse of Gen 3, awaiting liberation)
stenazomen (we groan -- 8:23; shared with creation, 8:22, and the Spirit, 8:26; the universal longing for completion and redemption)
hypernikomen (we more than conquer -- 8:37; the super-victory; not merely surviving but overwhelming through the love of Christ)

chapters.title: The Anguish of Israel and the Sovereignty of God
chapters.synopsis: Romans 9 opens with Paul's anguished lament: 'I have great sorrow and unceasing anguish in my heart. For I could wish that I myself were accursed (anathema) and cut off from Christ for the sake of my
hebrew_terms: anathema (accursed/devoted to destruction -- 9:3; the Hebrew cherem; Paul would accept being cut off from Christ for Israel's sake; echoes Moses' intercession in Exod 32:32)
hyiothesia (adoption -- 9:4; Israel's status as God's adopted son; 'Israel is my firstborn son,' Exod 4:22)
doxa (glory -- 9:4; the shekinah, the visible divine presence that dwelt in the tabernacle and temple)
diatheke (covenant -- 9:4; plural: the multiple covenants God made with Israel: Abrahamic, Sinaitic, Davidic, new covenant)
ekloge (election/choice -- 9:11; God's sovereign selection; 'in order that God's purpose of election might continue')
eleos (mercy -- 9:15-16, 18, 23; the divine prerogative: 'I will have mercy on whom I have mercy'; the foundation of all salvation)
skeuos (vessel -- 9:21-23; the potter's vessel; humanity as clay in God's hands; vessels of wrath and vessels of mercy)
hypomone (patience/endurance -- 9:22; God 'endured with much patience vessels of wrath'; the divine forbearance that delays judgment)
lithos proskommatos (stone of stumbling -- 9:32-33; Christ as the stumbling stone for Israel; from Isa 8:14 and 28:16)
katalemma (remnant -- 9:27; the Isaianic remnant principle: only a remnant of Israel will be saved; judgment with hope)

chapters.title: Christ the End of the Law -- The Word Is Near
chapters.synopsis: Romans 10 presents the gospel's universal accessibility. Paul restates his desire for Israel's salvation (10:1) and diagnoses their failure: 'They have a zeal for God, but not according to knowledge'
hebrew_terms: telos nomou (the end/goal of the law -- 10:4; telos can mean 'end' (termination) or 'goal' (fulfillment); Christ is both: the termination of law-righteousness and the fulfillment of law's purpose)
dikaiosyne ek pisteos (righteousness from faith -- 10:6; the righteousness Paul commends; contrasted with the self-achieved righteousness Israel pursued)
rhema (word/utterance -- 10:8; the spoken, proclaimed word; 'the word of faith that we proclaim'; the gospel as oral proclamation)
homologeo (to confess -- 10:9-10; public declaration: 'Jesus is Lord'; the baptismal confession of the early church)
kyrios (Lord -- 10:9, 12-13; the title that identifies Jesus with YHWH; 'everyone who calls on the name of the Lord' -- Joel 2:32 applied to Christ)
epikaleisthai (to call upon -- 10:12-14; to invoke the name of the Lord; the act of faith expressed in prayer; universal access)
keryssein (to preach/proclaim -- 10:14-15; the herald's announcement; the gospel requires human messengers)
akoe (hearing/report -- 10:16-17; 'faith comes from hearing'; the auditory nature of faith; the gospel is heard, not discovered)
parazelo (to make jealous -- 10:19; God will use the Gentiles' faith to provoke Israel to jealousy; quoting Deut 32:21)
apeitheo (to disobey/disbelieve -- 10:21; Israel's posture: 'a disobedient and contrary people'; not ignorance but willful rejection)

chapters.title: The Olive Tree and the Mystery -- All Israel Will Be Saved
chapters.synopsis: Romans 11 resolves the tension of chapters 9-10 with three arguments: (1) God has NOT rejected Israel -- a remnant remains, chosen by grace (11:1-10). (2) Israel's stumbling has a purpose -- to bring
hebrew_terms: leimma (remnant -- 11:5; the preserved core; 'at the present time there is a remnant chosen by grace'; the Isaianic remnant theology)
ekloge charitos (election of grace -- 11:5; the remnant exists by grace, not works; election and grace are inseparable)
porosis (hardening -- 11:7, 25; the judicial blinding of the majority of Israel; partial and temporary, not total or permanent)
aparche (firstfruits -- 11:16; if the firstfruits are holy, the whole lump is holy; the patriarchs' holiness consecrates the nation)
rhiza (root -- 11:16-18; the patriarchal root from which both natural and grafted branches grow; the foundation of the olive tree)
agrielaios (wild olive -- 11:17, 24; the Gentile believers grafted into Israel's olive tree; wild branches sharing the cultivated root's nourishment)
mysterion (mystery -- 11:25; the revealed secret: Israel's partial hardening until the Gentile fullness; not unknowable but now disclosed)
pleroma ton ethnon (fullness of the Gentiles -- 11:25; the complete number of Gentile believers who will come in before Israel's restoration)
pas Israel (all Israel -- 11:26; the debated phrase: ethnic Israel as a whole? the elect of Israel? all the people of God?)
anerevneta / anexichniastoi (unsearchable / inscrutable -- 11:33; God's judgments cannot be exhaustively investigated; his ways cannot be fully tracked)

chapters.title: Living Sacrifices, Renewed Minds, and Love Fulfilling the Law
chapters.synopsis: The great 'therefore' (oun) of 12:1 pivots from theology to practice: 'I appeal to you therefore, brothers, by the mercies of God, to present your bodies as a living sacrifice.' The renewed mind (12:2
hebrew_terms: parastesai (to present/offer -- 12:1; the priestly term for offering sacrifice; the same verb used in 6:13 for presenting members as instruments of righteousness)
thysia zosa (living sacrifice -- 12:1; unlike animal sacrifices that die; the believer's entire life as continuous offering; a living death-to-self)
logike latreia (spiritual/rational worship -- 12:1; worship that engages the mind; not ecstatic but thoughtful, not ritualistic but wholistic)
metamorphousthe (be transformed -- 12:2; the verb from which 'metamorphosis' derives; continuous transformation, present tense imperative)
anakainosis tou noos (renewal of the mind -- 12:2; the new mind replacing the debased mind (adokimon noun) of 1:28; the cognitive reversal of the fall)
charisma (grace-gift -- 12:6; the Spirit's gifts distributed within the body; not earned abilities but granted capacities)
agape anypokritos (love without hypocrisy -- 12:9; genuine, unfeigned love; the opposite of the theatrical mask [hypokrisis])
exousia (authority -- 13:1; the governing powers; 'there is no authority except from God'; the political application of divine sovereignty)
pleroma nomou (fulfillment of the law -- 13:10; love as the sum and completion of all Torah commands; the law's telos achieved through love)
endysasthe ton kyrion (put on the Lord -- 13:14; clothe yourselves with Christ; the ethical 'putting on' that corresponds to baptismal 'putting on' in Gal 3:27)

chapters.title: The Weak and the Strong -- Welcome One Another as Christ Welcomed You
chapters.synopsis: Romans 14:1-15:13 addresses the pastoral crisis in the Roman church: division between 'the weak' (those who observe food laws and holy days, likely Jewish believers maintaining Torah practices) and 't
hebrew_terms: astheneo (to be weak -- 14:1; weakness of conscience, not of faith per se; the person who feels bound by dietary or calendar restrictions)
proslambanesthe (welcome/accept -- 14:1; 15:7; take to yourself, receive as a companion; not reluctant tolerance but genuine embrace)
dialogismoi (disputes/quarrels -- 14:1; literally 'reasonings'; not to welcome the weak person in order to argue about opinions)
koinos (common/unclean -- 14:14; in itself nothing is unclean; 'I am persuaded in the Lord Jesus that nothing is unclean in itself')
basileia tou theou (kingdom of God -- 14:17; the rare Pauline use of this phrase; defined as righteousness, peace, and joy in the Spirit)
oikodomeo (to build up/edify -- 14:19; the architectural metaphor: pursue what builds up the community, not what tears it down)
proskomma (stumbling block -- 14:13, 20; the obstacle placed in a brother's path; do not destroy by your food the one for whom Christ died)
hypostasis (conviction/faith -- 14:22-23; 'whatever does not proceed from faith (pistis) is sin'; personal conviction before God)
allelous (one another -- 15:7; the reciprocal pronoun of mutual acceptance; not one group tolerating another but both welcoming each other)
elpis (hope -- 15:13; the 'God of hope' who fills believers with all joy and peace in believing; the final word of the ethical section)

chapters.title: Paul's Mission, the Church in Rome, and the Crushing of Satan
chapters.synopsis: The letter's final section reveals Paul's missionary vision: he is 'a minister of Christ Jesus to the Gentiles, serving the gospel of God as a priest (hierourgounta)' (15:16). His priestly offering is
hebrew_terms: leitourgos Christou Iesou (minister/priest of Christ Jesus -- 15:16; the priestly language for Paul's apostolic ministry; the gospel proclamation as liturgical service)
hierourgounta (serving as a priest -- 15:16; a hapax legomenon; Paul functions as a priest offering the Gentiles to God; the missionary offering)
prosphora ton ethnon (the offering of the Gentiles -- 15:16; the Gentiles themselves are the sacrifice Paul presents; sanctified by the Spirit)
semeion (sign -- 15:19; miraculous signs that accompanied Paul's preaching; the divine validation of the apostolic mission)
koinonia (sharing/partnership -- 15:26; the financial collection as an expression of spiritual solidarity; Gentile money for Jewish saints)
diakonos (deacon/servant -- 16:1; Phoebe's title; a recognized office in the early church; she is also called 'prostatis' [patron/benefactor])
synergoi (co-workers -- 16:3, 9, 21; Paul's extensive network of ministry partners; the collaborative nature of the apostolic mission)
syntribe (crushing -- 16:20; the verb from Gen 3:15 LXX; the serpent's head crushed; the protoevangelium fulfilled through the church)
mysterion (mystery -- 16:25; the final use; the mystery 'kept secret for long ages but now disclosed'; the Gentile inclusion as cosmic secret now revealed)
mono sopho theo (the only wise God -- 16:27; the doxological climax: glory to the one God whose wisdom designed the cosmic plan of salvation)

chapters.title: The Wisdom of the Cross vs. the Rulers of This Age
chapters.synopsis: Paul opens by addressing the Corinthian factions: some claim Paul, others Apollos, others Cephas, others Christ (1:10-17). He rejects this personality cult by pointing to the cross as God's wisdom --
hebrew_terms: archontes tou aionos toutou (rulers of this age -- the spiritual-political powers who orchestrated the crucifixion; debate whether human, spiritual, or both)
sophia (wisdom -- God's hidden plan vs. the world's reasoning; in Prov 8:22-31, Wisdom personified participated in creation)
mysterion (mystery -- not a puzzle to solve but a secret plan now revealed; used 6x in 1 Cor, rooted in Dan 2:18-19 LXX raz/mysterion)
dynamis (power -- the cross as God's power, inverting human expectations of what power looks like)
moria (foolishness -- the apparent absurdity of a crucified Messiah; from which English 'moron' derives)
psychikos (natural/soulish -- the unspiritual person who cannot discern spiritual realities; from psyche, 'soul')
pneumatikos (spiritual -- one who has received the Spirit and can discern God's wisdom; from pneuma, 'spirit/breath')
naos (temple/sanctuary -- the inner shrine where God dwells; Paul applies it to the church community and the individual body)

chapters.title: Purity, Judgment, and the Body -- Saints Who Judge Angels
chapters.synopsis: Paul addresses a shocking case of sexual immorality: a man is living with his father's wife (stepmother), and the church is 'puffed up' rather than grieving (5:1-2). Paul commands them to 'deliver thi
hebrew_terms: krinein angelous (to judge angels -- believers will participate in the eschatological judgment of rebellious angelic beings; cf. Psalm 82)
paradounai to satana (to deliver to Satan -- expulsion from the covenant community into the hostile spiritual realm outside; cf. Job 1-2, 1 Tim 1:20)
naos tou hagiou pneumatos (temple of the Holy Spirit -- the individual body as sacred divine dwelling; naos = inner sanctuary, not hieron = temple complex)
porneia (sexual immorality -- broad term covering all illicit sexual activity; from porne, 'prostitute'; source of English 'pornography')
soma (body -- not merely physical matter but the whole person in relationship; Paul redeems the body from Greek contempt)
agorazein (to buy/purchase -- 'you were bought with a price' [6:20]; marketplace language for redemption, linked to the go'el tradition)
exousia (authority/right -- 'the wife does not have authority over her own body' [7:4]; mutual submission in marriage)

chapters.title: Idol Food, Demons, and the Angels Who Watch
chapters.synopsis: Paul addresses three interconnected issues: eating meat sacrificed to idols (chs. 8-10), his apostolic rights and example of self-denial (ch. 9), and worship order including the Lord's Supper (ch. 11)
hebrew_terms: daimonia (demons -- in Paul's usage, the spiritual beings behind pagan worship; the LXX of Deut 32:17 uses daimoniois for the Hebrew shedim)
eidolothyton (idol-meat -- meat sacrificed to an idol; a compound from eidolon [idol] + thyo [sacrifice])
koinonia (fellowship/participation -- sharing in something at a deep level; used for both the Lord's table and demonic fellowship)
diakonos (servant/minister -- Paul's self-description; from which English 'deacon' derives)
exousia (authority/power -- the 'authority' on a woman's head [11:10]; debated whether it means a symbol of authority or the woman's own authority)
dia tous angelous (because of the angels -- the reason for head coverings in worship; likely referencing angelic observers and/or the Watcher tradition)
anamnesis (remembrance -- 'do this in remembrance of me' [11:24-25]; not mere mental recall but active re-presentation of Christ's death)

chapters.title: Spiritual Gifts, the Body, and the Way of Love
chapters.synopsis: Paul addresses the Corinthians' questions about 'spiritual gifts' (pneumatika, 12:1). He establishes that all genuine gifts come from the same Spirit (12:4-11), lists nine representative gifts (wisdom
hebrew_terms: charismata (grace-gifts -- gifts given by God's charis [grace]; from which 'charismatic' derives; distinct from natural talents)
pneumatika (spiritual things/gifts -- the Corinthians' word; Paul reframes these as charismata to emphasize divine origin rather than personal achievement)
soma Christou (body of Christ -- the church as Christ's physical presence on earth; each member has a unique function)
agape (love -- self-giving, sacrificial love; not eros [romantic] or philia [friendship]; the defining characteristic of Christian community)
diakriseis pneumaton (distinguishing/discerning of spirits -- the ability to determine whether a spiritual manifestation is from God, from the flesh, or from hostile spiritual beings)
glossolalia (speaking in tongues -- from glossa [tongue/language] + lalein [to speak]; debated whether human languages [Acts 2] or ecstatic speech)
propheteia (prophecy -- speaking God's word into a specific situation; in Paul, primarily edification, encouragement, and consolation [14:3])

chapters.title: Resurrection and the Destruction of Every Power
chapters.synopsis: Chapter 15 is the most sustained argument for bodily resurrection in the New Testament and contains one of the most important divine council passages: Christ destroys 'every rule and every authority a
hebrew_terms: arche (rule/ruler/beginning -- in 15:24, the cosmic rulers; the same word used for spiritual powers in Eph 1:21, 3:10, 6:12; Col 1:16, 2:15)
exousia (authority -- cosmic spiritual authorities; paired with arche in the Pauline power vocabulary)
dynamis (power -- the third term in Paul's triad of hostile cosmic powers; also 'miracle' in 12:10, 28-29)
aparche (firstfruits -- the first portion of the harvest offered to God, guaranteeing the full harvest to come; Christ's resurrection guarantees ours)
psychikon soma (natural/soulish body -- the current body animated by psyche [soul/life]; mortal and perishable)
pneumatikon soma (spiritual body -- the resurrection body animated by pneuma [Spirit]; immortal and imperishable; NOT non-physical)
katargeo (to destroy/abolish/render powerless -- the verb for Christ's destruction of the cosmic powers [15:24, 26]; also used in 2:6 for the rulers being 'brought to nothing')
maranatha (Our Lord, come! -- Aramaic liturgical cry [16:22]; maran = our Lord + atha = come; one of the earliest Christian prayers)

chapters.title: The Foolishness of God and the Shame of the Wise
chapters.synopsis: Paul opens his letter to the fractured Corinthian church by confronting their personality cults (1:10-17): factions claiming Paul, Apollos, Cephas, or Christ as their patron-leader. This mirrors the G
hebrew_terms: sophia (wisdom -- Greek philosophical pursuit; Paul redefines it as the cross, inverting the entire Hellenistic value system)
moria (foolishness/madness -- the cross appears as sheer absurdity to Gentile philosophical culture; root of English 'moron')
dynamis (power -- the cross as God's power; the same term used for spiritual might in the divine council hierarchy; cf. Ps 103:20 LXX)
skandalon (stumbling block/trap -- the cross as offensive to Jewish messianic expectations of a conquering Davidic king)
klesis (calling/vocation -- God's sovereign summons; 1:26 'consider your calling' -- the called ones are the nobodies, not the somebodies)
ta me onta (the things that are not -- philosophical language for non-existence; God creates ex nihilo in both cosmology and soteriology)
ta onta (the things that are -- the existing power structures: wise, strong, noble; all nullified by God's counter-strategy)
kauchaomai (to boast/glory -- 1:29, 31; all boasting must be 'in the Lord,' not in human leaders or accomplishments)
hagiasmos (sanctification -- 1:30; Christ is our sanctification; the process by which God sets apart a people for himself)
apolytrosis (redemption -- 1:30; the slave-market metaphor; Christ purchases believers out of bondage to the powers of this age)

chapters.title: The Hidden Mystery and the Rulers of This Age
chapters.synopsis: Paul now reveals the existence of a genuine divine wisdom -- but one that operates on entirely different terms than human philosophy. He came to Corinth 'in weakness and in fear and much trembling' (2
hebrew_terms: archontes tou aionos toutou (rulers of this age -- the spiritual powers governing the present evil age under the Deut 32 allotment; the term archon is used for both human officials and cosmic powers in Second Temple literature)
mysterion (mystery/secret -- from the Hebrew raz and Aramaic raz in Daniel 2:18-19, 27-30; not a puzzle to solve but a divine plan hidden in the council and now revealed through the Spirit)

kyrios tes doxes (Lord of glory -- a title applied to YHWH enthroned in the heavenly court in 1 Enoch 22:14, 25:3, 27:3-5; Paul transfers it to the crucified Christ, identifying him with the God of the divine assembly)

katargoumenon (being brought to nothing/rendered inoperative -- present passive participle indicating an ongoing process; the same verb used in 15:24 for the destruction of all spiritual rule and authority)
pro ton aionon (before the ages -- the temporal frame of the mystery's decree; implies God's plan preceded the spiritual powers' rebellion and was designed to entrap them)
pneumatikos (spiritual person -- one who possesses God's Spirit and can therefore access divine council revelation; contrasted with psychikos in 2:14-15)

psychikos (natural/soulish person -- one limited to human faculties without the Spirit; literally 'soul-ish'; such a person 'does not accept the things of the Spirit of God' and 'is not able to understand them' because they are 'spiritually discerned')

ta bathe tou theou (the depths/deep things of God -- 2:10; the innermost deliberations of the divine council, accessible only through the Spirit; cf. Job 11:7, 'Can you find out the deep things of God?')
nous Christou (the mind of Christ -- 2:16; believers possess Christ's own perspective on reality, enabling them to evaluate all things from the divine council's viewpoint)

teleios (mature/complete -- 2:6; the spiritually mature who can receive the hidden wisdom; in the mystery religions this was an initiation term, but Paul democratizes it: maturity comes through the Spirit, not through elite ritual)

chapters.title: God's Temple, God's Field -- The Apostolic Foundation
chapters.synopsis: Paul applies the cruciform wisdom theology of chapters 1-2 to the practical life of the Corinthian church. He begins by rebuking them: despite having the Spirit, they are behaving as sarkinos (fleshly
hebrew_terms: naos theou (temple/sanctuary of God -- the inner shrine, the holy of holies where YHWH's presence dwells; distinct from hieron, the broader temple complex; Paul applies it to the community, not a building)
themelion (foundation -- 3:11; Christ is the only valid foundation; in the LXX, used for the foundation of the temple and of Zion itself; cf. Isa 28:16)
dokimazo (to test/prove -- 3:13; the eschatological fire that tests the quality of each builder's work; a metallurgical metaphor for purification by divine judgment)
synergoi theou (fellow-workers of God -- 3:9; ministers are not competing authorities but co-laborers in God's project; the term emphasizes divine initiative and human instrumentality)
sarkinos (fleshly/made of flesh -- 3:1; not merely 'sinful' but operating according to the old-age categories of power, status, and rivalry rather than the Spirit's wisdom)
theatron (theater/spectacle -- 4:9; the apostles as public display of cruciform existence, watched by the cosmos, angels, and humanity; the divine council and all creation observe)
oikonomos (steward/household manager -- 4:1; apostles manage the master's household and are entrusted with mysteries; drawn from the royal court where stewards administered the king's affairs)
hyperphroneo (to think beyond what is written -- 4:6; the Corinthian disease of inflated self-estimation, evaluating themselves by worldly metrics rather than cruciform ones)
perikatharma (refuse/scum -- 4:13; Paul describes apostles as the 'scum of the world, the refuse of all things'; the term was used for human scapegoats in Athenian purification rituals)
peripsema (offscouring -- 4:13; paired with perikatharma; in some traditions used for expiatory offerings; the apostolic life as living sacrifice)

chapters.title: The Gospel Received: Christ Died, Was Buried, Was Raised
chapters.synopsis: Paul opens the resurrection argument by grounding it in the earliest Christian creed -- a pre-Pauline tradition he 'received' (paralambanein) and 'delivered' (paradidonai) to the Corinthians (15:3). T
hebrew_terms: paradidomi (delivered/handed down -- technical term for the transmission of authoritative oral tradition; the same verb used for Judas 'handing over' Jesus [Matt 26:15] and for God 'handing over' humans to sin [Rom 1:24]; here it describes the faithful chain of creedal transmission)

paralambanein (received -- the correlate of paradidomi; together they form the technical vocabulary for tradition transmission in both rabbinic Judaism [qibbel/masar] and Hellenistic schools; Paul received this creed from the Jerusalem apostles, likely within 3-5 years of the crucifixion)

egeiro (raised -- the dominant NT verb for resurrection; passive voice implies God as the agent: God raised Christ; distinguished from anistemi [to rise/stand up]; the passive 'was raised' emphasizes divine action, not human achievement)

ophthe (appeared/was seen -- aorist passive of horao [to see]; used for visible, objective appearances, not subjective visions; the same verb used for theophanies in the LXX [Gen 12:7, 'the LORD appeared to Abram']; these are real encounters, not hallucinations)

kenon (empty/vain -- used twice in 15:14 and 15:17; if Christ is not raised, both the preaching and the faith are kenon -- devoid of content, hollow, worthless; related to kenosis [self-emptying] in Phil 2:7)

pseudomartyres tou theou (false witnesses of God -- if the resurrection did not happen, the apostles are not merely mistaken but liars who testified falsely about what God did; the stakes could not be higher)

ektroma (untimely birth/miscarriage -- Paul's self-description in 15:8; a violent, disturbing term suggesting his apostleship was unnatural, forced, born out of due time; possibly referencing the trauma of his conversion and the abnormality of seeing the risen Christ after the ascension)

kata tas graphas (according to the Scriptures -- used twice in the creed [15:3, 4]; the death and resurrection are not random events but fulfillments of the OT pattern; 'the Scriptures' likely include Isa 53, Hos 6:2, Ps 16:10, and the broader typological pattern of death-and-restoration in Israel's story)

koinonia (fellowship/communion -- though not explicitly in 15:1-19, the creedal transmission implies koinonia: Paul shares in the same gospel he received; the resurrection creates a community of witness that spans from Jerusalem to Corinth)

chapters.title: Then Comes the End: The Destruction of Every Rule and Authority
chapters.synopsis: Having established that Christ's resurrection is historically attested and theologically non-negotiable, Paul now unveils the cosmic scope of what that resurrection sets in motion. Christ has been rai
hebrew_terms: aparche (firstfruits -- the first sheaf of the grain harvest, offered to God on the day after the Sabbath during Passover week [Lev 23:10-11]; by presenting the first portion, the farmer consecrated the entire harvest; Christ's resurrection is the aparche that guarantees the resurrection of all who belong to him)

tagma (order/rank/arrangement -- a military term for a unit or division; Paul uses it for the sequential 'order' of resurrection: Christ first, then believers at the parousia, then the end; the resurrection unfolds according to divine strategy, not chaos)

katargeo (to destroy/abolish/render powerless/bring to nothing -- Paul's verb for what Christ does to every hostile power [15:24] and to death [15:26]; the same verb used in 2:6 for the rulers of this age being 'brought to nothing'; it means to make completely inoperative, to strip of all force and authority)

arche (rule/ruler/principality -- the first term in Paul's triad of cosmic powers [15:24]; used for spiritual authorities in Eph 1:21, 3:10, 6:12; Col 1:16, 2:10, 15; these are the divine council rebels who govern the nations under the Deut 32:8 allotment)

exousia (authority -- the second term in the power triad; authority delegated by God but exercised in rebellion; used for both spiritual [Eph 6:12, Col 2:15] and earthly [Rom 13:1] authorities; Christ's resurrection strips them of their illegitimate claim)

dynamis (power -- the third term; raw cosmic force wielded by the hostile powers; also used positively for God's power [1:18, 24] and for miracles [12:10]; context determines whether the reference is to divine or rebellious power)

eschatos echthros (last enemy -- death personified as the final opponent in the cosmic war; 'last' not merely in chronological sequence but in significance: death is the most fundamental weapon of the hostile powers, the foundation on which all their authority rests; destroy death, and every other power crumbles)

baptizomenoi hyper ton nekron (those being baptized on behalf of the dead -- 15:29; one of the most debated phrases in the NT; over 40 interpretations proposed; most likely refers to a practice of vicarious baptism for deceased believers, which Paul neither endorses nor condemns but uses as evidence that even the Corinthians' own practice implies belief in bodily resurrection)

dei gar auton basileuein (for he must reign -- the divine necessity [dei] of Christ's messianic reign; drawn from Psalm 110:1 where YHWH seats the Messiah at his right hand; the 'must' indicates this is not optional but part of the divine council decree established before the ages)

chapters.title: Victory Over Death: The Imperishable Body and the Final Triumph
chapters.synopsis: Paul now addresses the inevitable question: 'How are the dead raised? With what kind of body do they come?' (15:35). He answers with a series of analogies and contrasts. First, the seed analogy: a gra
hebrew_terms: soma pneumatikon (spiritual body -- NOT a non-physical body but a body animated and governed by the Holy Spirit [pneuma]; the opposite of soma psychikon [body animated by natural life]; the resurrection body is more physical, not less -- it is material existence raised to a higher order, imperishable and immortal)

soma psychikon (natural/soulish body -- the present body animated by psyche [soul/natural life]; subject to decay, weakness, and death; the 'bare kernel' that must be transformed; psychikos in Paul always means 'unspiritual' or 'merely natural' -- limited to created life without the Spirit's animation)

phthora (corruption/decay/perishability -- the fundamental condition of the present body; from phtheiro, 'to corrupt/destroy'; the resurrection body is aphtharsia [incorruption/imperishability], the negation of phthora; this is not merely about aging but about the entire created order's subjection to futility [Rom 8:20-21])

aphtharsia (imperishability/incorruption -- the quality of the resurrection body; what the mortal 'puts on' at the last trumpet [15:53-54]; the negation of phthora; this word also describes God himself in Rom 1:23 ['the glory of the immortal/imperishable God']; the resurrection body shares in God's own quality of indestructibility)

mysterion (mystery -- a secret plan of God now revealed; not a puzzle to solve but a divine strategy previously hidden; Paul uses it for the revelation that not all will die but all will be transformed [15:51]; the same word used in 2:7 for the hidden wisdom God decreed 'before the ages'; the mystery of resurrection is part of the larger mystery of God's plan to defeat the powers)

nike (victory -- the triumphal conclusion: 'Death is swallowed up in victory' [15:54]; 'O death, where is your victory?' [15:55]; from nikao, 'to conquer'; the resurrection is not escape from the battlefield but total conquest of the enemy; God 'gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ' [15:57])

kentron (sting/goad -- 'O death, where is your sting?' [15:55]; the sting of death is sin, and the power (dynamis) of sin is the law [15:56]; the three enemies form a chain: the law exposes sin, sin produces death, death is the sting; Christ breaks the chain at every link)

salpinx (trumpet -- 'at the last trumpet' [15:52]; in the OT, the trumpet sounds for theophany [Exod 19:16], for war [Josh 6:5], for the Day of the Lord [Joel 2:1], and for the gathering of God's people [Isa 27:13]; 'the last trumpet' signals the final divine intervention that ends the present age)

eikon (image -- 'as we have borne the image [eikon] of the man of dust, we shall also bear the image of the man of heaven' [15:49]; connects to Gen 1:26-27 where humans are made in God's image [tselem]; the resurrection restores and surpasses the original image-bearing vocation)

chapters.title: The God of This Age and the New Creation Ministry
chapters.synopsis: Paul opens with a theology of suffering and comfort: 'the God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our affliction' (1:3-4). He references a near-death experience in Asia (possibly the Ephesian riot
hebrew_terms: ho theos tou aionos toutou (the god of this age -- Satan as the ruling spiritual power of the present world-system; an astounding title that presupposes divine council hierarchy)
eikon tou theou (image of God -- Christ as the perfect image/representation of God; connects to Gen 1:26-27; Col 1:15)
kaine ktisis (new creation -- the eschatological reality inaugurated in Christ; from kainos [new in quality] + ktisis [creation]; cf. Isa 65:17)
katallage (reconciliation -- God's initiative to restore broken relationship; from katallasso [to change from enmity to friendship])
ostrakinois skeusin (jars of clay -- fragile earthenware vessels; metaphor for the weakness of apostolic ministers who carry divine treasure)
paraklesis (comfort/encouragement -- used 10 times in 1:3-7; from parakaleo [to call alongside]; the same root as parakletos [Advocate/Comforter])
doxa (glory -- the radiant manifestation of God's presence; the new covenant ministry has 'much more glory' than the old [3:9-11])

chapters.title: Holiness, Generosity, and Separation from Belial
chapters.synopsis: Paul urges the Corinthians not to receive God's grace 'in vain' (6:1) and catalogs his apostolic sufferings (6:3-10). He then issues a striking call to holiness: 'Do not be unequally yoked with unbeli
hebrew_terms: Beliar/Belial (worthlessness/wickedness -- in Second Temple literature, a proper name for Satan or the chief demon; used in DSS War Scroll, Testament of Levi, Jubilees)
heterozygountes (unequally yoked -- from heteros [different] + zygos [yoke]; metaphor from Deut 22:10, prohibiting yoking an ox and donkey together)
naos theou zontos (temple of the living God -- the church community as God's dwelling; 'living God' distinguishes YHWH from dead idols)
charis (grace -- used 18x in chs. 8-9; means both 'gift' and 'generosity'; the theological foundation for the collection)
metanoia (repentance -- 'godly grief produces repentance that leads to salvation without regret' [7:10]; from meta [change] + nous [mind])
haplotes (generosity/sincerity -- 'your generous gift' [9:11]; literally 'singleness' of heart, without duplicity)

chapters.title: The Third Heaven, the Thorn, and Power in Weakness
chapters.synopsis: Paul shifts to a confrontational tone, defending his apostleship against the 'super-apostles' (hyperlian apostoloi, 11:5; 12:11) who have infiltrated Corinth. He wages spiritual warfare: 'the weapons
hebrew_terms: tritos ouranos (third heaven -- the highest heaven, the dwelling place of God; presupposes a multi-layered cosmic geography; equated with paradise)
paradeisos (paradise -- a Persian loanword [pairidaeza = walled garden]; used in LXX for Eden [Gen 2:8]; the eschatological dwelling of the righteous)
angelos Satana (messenger/angel of Satan -- a demonic agent assigned to afflict Paul; the 'thorn in the flesh')
skolops te sarki (thorn in the flesh -- skolops can mean 'thorn,' 'stake,' or 'splinter'; the nature of Paul's affliction is unknown and much debated)
hyperlian apostoloi (super-apostles -- Paul's sarcastic term for the rival missionaries in Corinth; from hyper [above] + lian [exceedingly])
ochyromatōn (strongholds -- military fortifications; metaphor for ideological and spiritual structures opposed to God [10:4])
dynamis en astheneia teleitai (power is perfected in weakness -- the paradoxical principle of divine operation; God's power manifests most fully through human fragility)

2cor_glory.name: The Unveiled Glory and New Creation

chapters.title: From Glory to Greater Glory — The Fading Veil and the Transforming Spirit
chapters.synopsis: Paul begins by contrasting letters of recommendation -- the currency of the 'super-apostles' -- with the living letter written by the Spirit of the living God on human hearts (3:1-3). This launches hi
hebrew_terms: doxa (glory -- the visible radiance of God's presence and character; from Hebrew kavod, the weighty splendor of YHWH; the old covenant had doxa, but the new covenant's doxa is surpassing and permanent)

diakonia (ministry/service -- used to describe both the old covenant ministry of Moses [3:7-9] and the new covenant ministry of the Spirit; from diakonos [servant]; the contrast is between a diakonia of death [tou thanatou] and a diakonia of the Spirit [tou pneumatos])

katoptrizomenoi (beholding as in a mirror -- from katoptron [mirror]; the present participle suggests an ongoing process of gazing at the Lord's glory; some scholars translate 'reflecting' rather than 'beholding,' but the context favors active contemplation that produces transformation)

metamorphoo (to transform/transfigure -- from meta [change] + morphe [form]; the same verb used for Jesus' transfiguration [Matt 17:2; Mark 9:2]; believers undergo a progressive transfiguration into Christ's image through beholding his glory)

kalymma (veil/covering -- from kalypto [to cover, conceal]; the veil on Moses' face [Exod 34:33-35] becomes a metaphor for the spiritual blindness that prevents Israel from recognizing the new covenant's glory; removed only 'in Christ' [3:14])

katargeo (to abolish/bring to nothing/render inoperative -- used 4 times in 3:7-14; the old covenant glory was 'being brought to nothing' [to katargoumenon]; critically, this same verb describes Christ abolishing all hostile rule and authority in 1 Cor 15:24 and death itself in 1 Cor 15:26)

gramma (letter -- the written code of the law; 'the letter kills, but the Spirit gives life' [3:6]; gramma refers not to Scripture itself but to the law as external code apart from the Spirit's transforming power)

pneuma kyriou (the Spirit of the Lord -- 'where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is freedom' [3:17]; the Spirit is the agent of the new covenant transformation, replacing the external code with internal reality)

eleutheria (freedom/liberty -- 'where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is freedom' [3:17]; from eleutheros [free]; the freedom from the veil, from the condemnation of the law, and from the blindness that conceals glory)

eikon (image -- 'being transformed into the same image' [3:18]; connects directly to Gen 1:26-27 [imago Dei] and to 4:4 where Christ is 'the image of God'; believers are being restored to the eikon that Adam bore and lost)

chapters.title: The God of This Age and the Treasure in Clay
chapters.synopsis: Chapter 4 moves from the glory of the new covenant to the cosmic conflict that opposes it. Paul does not 'lose heart' (4:1) because his ministry is characterized by transparency, not the deception of
hebrew_terms: ho theos tou aionos toutou (the god of this age -- Satan identified with the stunning title theos, the same word the LXX uses for the elohim of Psalm 82:1; he is not a co-equal deity but a rebellious member of the divine council exercising illegitimate authority over the present world-system; aion [age] distinguishes his temporary rule from the 'age to come')

eikon tou theou (the image of God -- Christ as the perfect, uncorrupted representation of God; connects to Gen 1:26-27 [the imago Dei] and Col 1:15 ['the image of the invisible God']; Satan blinds minds specifically to prevent recognition of Christ as the true eikon)

photismos (illumination/light -- from phos [light]; 'the light of the knowledge of the glory of God' [4:6]; the gospel is not merely information but photismos -- a divine act of illumination that shatters the blindness imposed by the god of this age)

ostrakinois skeusin (jars of clay -- ostrakinois from ostrakon [potsherd, earthenware]; skeusin from skeuos [vessel, container]; fragile pottery used for everyday storage; the paradox that infinite divine treasure is carried in vessels that can be shattered by a single blow)

anakainoo (to renew/make new -- 'our inner self is being renewed [anakainoutai] day by day' [4:16]; the present passive indicates ongoing divine action; the same root as kaine ktisis [new creation] in 5:17; daily renewal is the engine of eschatological transformation)

thlipsis (affliction/tribulation -- 'this light momentary affliction' [4:17]; Paul uses the word that in Jewish apocalyptic tradition describes the tribulation preceding the messianic age; his sufferings are labor pains of the new creation)

baros doxes (weight of glory -- 'preparing for us an eternal weight of glory (aionion baros doxes) beyond all comparison' [4:17]; a wordplay: the Hebrew kavod means both 'glory' and 'heaviness/weight'; Paul heaps up superlatives to describe eschatological glory)

ta blepomena / ta me blepomena (the things seen / the things not seen -- 'the things that are seen are transient, but the things that are unseen are eternal' [4:18]; the unseen realm is the domain of the divine council -- the permanent spiritual reality behind the transient material world)

proskairos (transient/temporary -- from pros [toward] + kairos [time, season]; the visible world is merely 'for a season'; its destiny is katargeo, the same abolishing that awaits the hostile powers of 1 Cor 15:24)

aionios (eternal/age-lasting -- the unseen realities are aionios, belonging to the 'age to come' rather than 'this age' ruled by the god of this age; the adjective marks the permanent quality of the divine council realm)

chapters.title: New Creation: The Ministry of Reconciliation
chapters.synopsis: Chapter 5 brings the argument of chapters 3-4 to its theological climax. Paul opens with the eschatological tension between the present mortal body -- 'our earthly tent' (5:1) -- and the resurrection
hebrew_terms: kaine ktisis (new creation -- from kainos [new in quality, unprecedented] + ktisis [creation, the act of creating]; not neos [new in time] but kainos [new in kind]; the eschatological reality inaugurated in Christ; every believer is a living instance of the new creation breaking into the old world-system ruled by the god of this age)

katalasso (to reconcile -- from kata [down, thoroughly] + allasso [to change, to exchange]; to change from enmity to friendship; God is the subject who reconciles; humanity is the object reconciled; the scope is cosmic: the kosmos itself is being reconciled [5:19])

bema (judgment seat -- the raised platform where a Roman magistrate dispensed justice; Paul appeared before Gallio's bema in Corinth [Acts 18:12]; Christ's bema is the eschatological tribunal where believers give account for their stewardship of the body and the gospel ministry)

presbeuo (to serve as ambassador -- from presbys [elder, senior]; the technical term for a diplomatic representative of a sovereign in foreign territory; believers are Christ's ambassadors operating within the domain of the god of this age, representing the kingdom that is reclaiming the nations)

hamartia (sin -- 'he made him to be sin [hamartian]' [5:21]; Paul uses the noun without the article, suggesting not individual transgressions but the cosmic reality of sin itself; Christ became the embodiment of the sin-problem so that believers could become the embodiment of divine righteousness)

dikaiosyne theou (the righteousness of God -- 'so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' [5:21]; not merely imputed legal status but the living embodiment of God's covenant faithfulness; believers become walking demonstrations of God's saving justice)

arrabon (guarantee/down payment/pledge -- from a Semitic loanword meaning 'earnest money'; the Spirit as the guarantee of the coming resurrection body [5:5]; a commercial metaphor: the deposit that assures the full payment will follow; cf. Eph 1:14)

skenos (tent/tabernacle -- 'our earthly tent' [5:1]; from the same root as skene [tabernacle/tent of meeting]; the mortal body as a temporary dwelling, echoing the wilderness tabernacle that preceded the permanent temple; the resurrection body is the permanent 'building from God')

oikodome ek theou (building from God -- 'we have a building from God, a house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens' [5:1]; the contrast: skenos [tent] vs. oikodome [solid building]; acheiropoieton [not made with hands] distinguishes the resurrection body from anything constructed in the present age)

chapters.title: Weapons of Warfare -- Demolishing Strongholds
